title: Introduce new "combined" subcolumn for JSON data type that combines literal and sub-object values
body:
### Changelog category (leave one):
- New Feature


### Changelog entry (a [user-readable short description](https://github.com/ClickHouse/ClickHouse/blob/master/docs/changelog_entry_guidelines.md) of the changes that goes into CHANGELOG.md):
Add combined subcolumn syntax `json.@path` for the `JSON` type that returns the literal value as `Dynamic` if the path holds a scalar, the sub-object as `Dynamic` if the path holds a nested object, or `NULL` if the path is absent.


### Documentation entry for user-facing changes

#### Reading JSON combined sub-columns

The `JSON` type supports reading a path as a **combined sub-column** using the special syntax `json.@some.path`.
A combined sub-column for a given path returns:
- The literal value stored at that path as `Dynamic`, if the path has a literal value.
- A JSON sub-object at that path as `Dynamic`, if the path has no literal value but has nested sub-paths.
- `NULL`, if neither a literal value nor any sub-paths exist for that path.

This is useful when a path may hold either a scalar value or a nested object across different rows, and is more convenient than separately querying the literal sub-column (`json.a`) and the sub-object sub-column (`json.^a`).

The following example compares all three sub-column types for path `a`:

```sql title="Query"
CREATE TABLE test (json JSON) ENGINE = Memory;
INSERT INTO test VALUES ('{"a" : 42, "b" : {"c" : 1, "d" : "Hello"}}'), ('{"a" : {"x": 1, "y": 2}, "b" : {"c" : 1}}'), ('{"c" : "World"}');
SELECT json FROM test;
```

```text title="Response"
┌─json────────────────────────────┐
│ {"a":42,"b":{"c":1,"d":"Hello"}}│
│ {"a":{"x":1,"y":2},"b":{"c":1}}│
│ {"c":"World"}                   │
└─────────────────────────────────┘
```

```sql title="Query"
SELECT
    json.a,
    dynamicType(json.a),
    json.^a,
    toTypeName(json.^a),
    json.@a,
    dynamicType(json.@a)
FROM test;
```

```text title="Response"
┌─json.a─┬─dynamicType(json.a)─┬─json.^a───────┬─toTypeName(json.^a)─┬─json.@a───────┬─dynamicType(json.@a)─┐
│ 42     │ Int64               │ {}            │ JSON                │ 42            │ Int64                │
│ NULL   │ None                │ {"x":1,"y":2} │ JSON                │ {"x":1,"y":2} │ JSON                 │
│ NULL   │ None                │ {}            │ JSON                │ NULL          │ None                 │
└────────┴─────────────────────┴───────────────┴─────────────────────┴───────────────┴──────────────────────┘
```

- Row 1: `a` holds a literal `42`. `json.a` returns it as `Dynamic(Int64)`, `json.^a` returns an empty sub-object `{}` (no nested keys under `a`), and `json.@a` returns the literal `42`.
- Row 2: `a` holds a nested object. `json.a` returns `NULL` (no literal at that path), `json.^a` returns the sub-object as `JSON`, and `json.@a` also returns the sub-object as `Dynamic(JSON)`.
- Row 3: `a` is absent entirely. Both `json.a` and `json.@a` return `NULL`, while `json.^a` returns an empty `{}`.


- [ ] Documentation is written (mandatory for new features)

<!---
Directly edit documentation source files in the "docs" folder with the same pull-request as code changes

or

Add a user-readable short description of the changes that should be added to docs.clickhouse.com below.

At a minimum, the following information should be added (but add more as needed).
- Motivation: Why is this function, table engine, etc. useful to ClickHouse users?

- Parameters: If the feature being added takes arguments, options or is influenced by settings, please list them below with a brief explanation.

- Example use: A query or command.
-->
author: Avogar

reviews.body: Cursor Bugbot has reviewed your changes and found 1 potential issue. Bugbot Autofix is OFF. To automatically fix reported issues with cloud agents, have a team admin enable autofix in the [Cursor dashboard](https://www.cursor.com/dashboard?tab=bugbot).

commits.message:
Introduce the `json.` combined subcolumn that merges the literal
path subcolumn and the sub-object subcolumn into a single access:
- returns the literal value as `Dynamic` if the path holds a scalar,
- returns the sub-object as `Dynamic` if the path holds a nested object,
- returns `NULL` if the path is absent entirely.

Implementation:
- Parser: handle `$`-prefixed identifiers in `ParserCompoundIdentifier`
  via the new `is_dollar_bareword` flag in `ExpressionElementParsers`.
- In-memory (Memory engine): `extractCombinedColumn` in `DataTypeObject`
  merges the literal and sub-object columns row by row.
- MergeTree (on-disk): new `SerializationObjectCombinedPath` reads the
  literal and sub-object substreams and merges them during deserialization,
  using `insert_only_rows_in_current_range_from_substreams_cache` to avoid
  stale cache interactions between the two substreams.
- `tupleElement` and `JSONExtract` function families extended to support
  the combined subcolumn path prefix.

Tests added in `03467`–`03470` covering parser round-trips, prefix
correctness, Memory/Compact/Wide MergeTree variants, and `Array(JSON)`.

Documentation added to `newjson.md` with a comparison example of all
three subcolumn types (`json.a`, `json.^a`, `json.`) side by side.
Merge branch 'master' of github.com:ClickHouse/ClickHouse into json-new-subcolumn
Unify some code for sub-object and combined subcolumns
Merge branch 'master' of github.com:ClickHouse/ClickHouse into json-new-subcolumn
Use @ instead of $ for JSON combined subcolumn prefix to avoid identifier ambiguity; add serialization pooling support for SerializationObjectCombinedPath
Fix bug in Variant offsets cache

title: Fix flaky test 03634_autopr_input_bytes_estimation
body:
The test sporadically fails with `MEMORY_LIMIT_EXCEEDED` (code 241) on `query_23`
(`SELECT * FROM test.hits WHERE URL LIKE '%google%' ORDER BY EventTime LIMIT 10`).

All 6 failures in the last 30 days show the same pattern: when CI randomizes
`max_bytes_before_external_sort=0` (disabling external sort), the full ORDER BY
sort happens in-memory and needs >4.66 GiB — exceeding the CI environment's memory
cap. The test already pins `max_bytes_before_external_group_by=0` to keep GROUP BY
in-memory (external spills inflate `ReadCompressedBytes` and break the estimation
check). This applies the same treatment to external sort and removes the memory cap
since the test measures byte estimation accuracy, not memory consumption.

Affected checks: arm_binary, amd_msan, amd_tsan — 10 distinct PRs in 30 days, never
on master.

### Changelog category (leave one):
- CI Fix or Improvement (changelog entry is not required)

### Changelog entry (a [user-readable short description](https://github.com/ClickHouse/ClickHouse/blob/master/docs/changelog_entry_guidelines.md) of the changes that goes into CHANGELOG.md):
...

### Documentation entry for user-facing changes

- [ ] Documentation is written (mandatory for new features)
author: groeneai

commits.message:
Fix flaky test 03634_autopr_input_bytes_estimation (MEMORY_LIMIT_EXCEEDED)

Pin max_bytes_before_external_sort=0, max_bytes_ratio_before_external_sort=0,
and max_memory_usage=0 to prevent MEMORY_LIMIT_EXCEEDED on query_23.

query_23 does SELECT * FROM test.hits with ORDER BY, which needs >4.66 GiB
for in-memory sorting. When CI randomizes max_bytes_before_external_sort=0
(disabling external sort), sorting stays fully in-memory and hits the CI
environment's ~4.66 GiB memory limit. All 6 failures in the last 30 days
show the same pattern: code 241 on query_23 with 4.66 GiB cap.

The test already pins max_bytes_before_external_group_by=0 to keep GROUP BY
in-memory (external group-by would inflate ReadCompressedBytes). This commit
applies the same treatment to external sort and removes the memory cap.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

title: Validate corrupted data in DDSketch deserialization
body:
## Summary

- Add validation in `DDSketchLogarithmicMapping::deserialize`, `DDSketchDenseStore::deserialize`, and `DDSketchDenseLogarithmic::deserialize` to reject corrupted binary data (non-finite values, negative counts, excessive bin counts, integer overflows)
- Add guards in `DDSketchDenseLogarithmic::changeMapping` to prevent infinite loops and handle degenerate bounds
- Add unit tests (11 new gtest cases) and a functional test with hex-encoded corrupted payloads

Closes #92393

### Changelog category (leave one):
- Bug Fix (user-visible misbehavior in an official stable release)

### Changelog entry (a [user-readable short description](https://github.com/ClickHouse/ClickHouse/blob/master/docs/changelog_entry_guidelines.md) of the changes that goes into CHANGELOG.md):
Validate corrupted data during `DDSketch` deserialization to prevent segfaults, exceptions, infinite loops, and OOM when reading corrupted `quantilesDD` aggregate function states.

### Documentation entry for user-facing changes

- [ ] Documentation is written (mandatory for new features)

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
author: alexey-milovidov

reviews.body: I've read the code and found it good.

commits.message:
Validate corrupted data in DDSketch deserialization

Corrupted binary data inserted into a `quantilesDD` aggregate function
column was accepted without validation. When the corrupted state was
later used in merge/read operations, it could cause segfaults,
exceptions, infinite loops, and OOM.

Add validation in `DDSketchLogarithmicMapping::deserialize`:
- `std::isfinite` checks for `gamma` and `offset`
- Validate derived `multiplier` is finite and non-zero
- Use `INCORRECT_DATA` error code instead of `BAD_ARGUMENTS`

Add validation in `DDSketchDenseStore::deserialize`:
- Limit `num_bins` / `num_non_empty_bins` to `max_bins_deserialize` (65536)
- Validate each `bin_count` is finite and non-negative
- Use `Int64` arithmetic in `getNewLength` to prevent integer overflow
- Add key range check in `extendRange` with `max_key_range` (65536)

Add validation in `DDSketchDenseLogarithmic::deserialize`:
- Validate `zero_count` is finite and non-negative
- Validate computed `count` is finite

Add guards in `DDSketchDenseLogarithmic::changeMapping`:
- Skip bins with zero count
- Skip bins where `lowerBound` produces non-finite or degenerate bounds
- Add iteration guard to prevent infinite loops in remapping

Closes #92393

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

title: Fix INSERT parser failing on double-parenthesized compound SELECT
body:
## Summary
- Fix the INSERT query parser to rewind on column list parse failure, instead of returning false immediately
- This fixes "Inconsistent AST formatting" exception when INSERT queries use compound SELECT with `INTERSECT`/`EXCEPT` operators
- When the AST formatter wraps nested unions in parentheses (producing e.g., `INSERT INTO t1 ((SELECT 1) EXCEPT ALL (SELECT 2))`), the parser now correctly handles the double parentheses

The root cause: the INSERT parser consumed the first `(` for column list parsing, but when the second `(` couldn't be parsed as a column name, it returned `false` instead of rewinding `pos` to try parsing `((SELECT ...)` as a compound SELECT query.

Closes https://github.com/ClickHouse/ClickHouse/issues/90925

CI report: https://s3.amazonaws.com/clickhouse-test-reports/json.html?REF=master&sha=bfd26cd518682a2051429b51714e9c8c62806f45&name_0=MasterCI&name_1=BuzzHouse%20%28amd_debug%29&name_1=BuzzHouse%20%28amd_debug%29

### Changelog category (leave one):
- CI Fix or Improvement (changelog entry is not required)

### Changelog entry (a [user-readable short description](https://github.com/ClickHouse/ClickHouse/blob/master/docs/changelog_entry_guidelines.md) of the changes that goes into CHANGELOG.md):
...

### Documentation entry for user-facing changes

- [ ] Documentation is written (mandatory for new features)

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
author: alexey-milovidov

commits.message:
Fix INSERT parser failing on double-parenthesized compound SELECT

When `ASTSelectWithUnionQuery::formatQueryImpl` formats a compound SELECT
(e.g., with `INTERSECT`/`EXCEPT`) that is a child of INSERT, it wraps
nested unions in parentheses for correctness. This can produce queries
like `INSERT INTO t1 ((SELECT 1) EXCEPT ALL (SELECT 2)) INTERSECT (SELECT 3)`.

The INSERT parser failed to re-parse such queries because when it
encountered `((SELECT ...`, it consumed the first `(` for the column
list check, then failed to parse `(` as a column name and returned false
immediately, instead of rewinding and letting the `(` be parsed as part
of a SELECT query.

The fix rewinds `pos` to before the `(` when column list parsing fails
entirely, matching the existing behavior when column parsing succeeds
but no closing `)` is found.

Closes https://github.com/ClickHouse/ClickHouse/issues/90925

https://s3.amazonaws.com/clickhouse-test-reports/json.html?REF=master&sha=bfd26cd518682a2051429b51714e9c8c62806f45&name_0=MasterCI&name_1=BuzzHouse%20%28amd_debug%29&name_1=BuzzHouse%20%28amd_debug%29

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

title: Reduce logging for `test_storage_iceberg_concurrent` to avoid OOM
body:
### Changelog category (leave one):
- Not for changelog (changelog entry is not required)

---

```
  Test: test_storage_iceberg_concurrent — 225 concurrent SELECT * against an IcebergS3 table, 15 GiB memory limit.

  Symptom: Server OOM-killed by the OS (log cuts off abruptly at 14:27:28, no graceful shutdown).

  Root cause: Excessive logging from the Parquet read path overwhelmed system.text_log, creating an unbounded in-memory backlog.

  The test runs at the global test log level. Three S3 loggers (AWSClient, ReadBufferFromS3, AsynchronousBoundedReadBuffer) produced ~83K LOG_TEST lines. But the dominant contributor was ParquetReadManager — 820K lines (83% of the entire log) at LOG_DEBUG,
  logging every scheduling decision, pointer advance, and "no work to do" non-event in the parallel Parquet reader's hot loop, multiplied by 225 concurrent queries.

  This produced a positive feedback loop in system.text_log: the in-memory queue grew faster than it could be flushed (1K → 25K → 74K → 111K → 146K → 367K entries), each flush took longer and generated more log entries, until memory was exhausted.

  Fix:
  1. Changed 24 LOG_DEBUG → LOG_TEST in ParquetReadManager (keeping 2 DEADLOCK-detection messages at LOG_DEBUG).
  2. Added per-logger trace-level overrides for the 3 S3 loggers in the test's config.
```

Original [failed check](https://d1k2gkhrlfqv31.cloudfront.net/clickhouse-test-reports-private/json.html?PR=46306&sha=c90551b9639c94d692c8edf38595332b934293d5&name_0=PR&name_1=Integration+tests+%28amd_asan_ubsan%2C+db+disk%2C+old+analyzer%2C+4%2F6%29)
author: nickitat

title: Fix logical error in HashJoin::getNonJoinedBlocks due to overlapping columns
body:
## Summary

- Fix a logical error exception in `HashJoin::getNonJoinedBlocks` caused by left-table column identifiers appearing in the right plan's header
- In edge cases (found by AST fuzzer), `AddedColumns` deduplicates overlapping columns but `getNonJoinedBlocks` expects the arithmetic sum, causing `Unexpected number of columns in result sample block`
- Add an `ExpressionStep` projection in `buildJoinQueryPlan` to remove non-key right columns overlapping with left column names before the join algorithm is created

Closes #85622
Closes #86528

## Test plan

- [x] Added test `03993_join_no_overlapping_columns_85622` reproducing the issue
- [ ] Verify test passes in CI (Debug/ASan builds where the assertion fires)
- [ ] Run AST fuzzer suite to confirm no regressions

### Changelog category (leave one):
- CI Fix or Improvement

### Changelog entry (a [user-readable short description](https://github.com/ClickHouse/ClickHouse/blob/master/docs/changelog_entry_guidelines.md) of the changes that goes into CHANGELOG.md):
Fix logical error exception `Unexpected number of columns in result sample block` in `HashJoin::getNonJoinedBlocks` when left-table column identifiers overlap with non-key right-side columns.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
author: alexey-milovidov

commits.message:
Fix logical error in `HashJoin::getNonJoinedBlocks` due to overlapping columns

In an edge case found by AST fuzzer, a left-table column identifier
(e.g., `__table1.key`) ends up in the right plan's header. When
`HashJoin` splits the right header into keys vs non-keys, this left-side
column lands in `sample_block_with_columns_to_add`. Then `AddedColumns`
deduplicates it (skips right columns matching left column names), but
`getNonJoinedBlocks` expects the arithmetic sum of left + right key +
columns_to_add to equal `result_sample_block` columns, causing:

    Unexpected number of columns in result sample block

The fix adds an `ExpressionStep` projection in `buildJoinQueryPlan` that
removes non-key right columns whose names overlap with left column names,
before the join algorithm is created.

Closes #85622
Closes #86528

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

title: Fix WriteBufferDecorator::cancelImpl writing to finalized buffer
body:
## Summary
- Fix `WriteBufferDecorator::cancelImpl` attempting to flush data to an already-finalized downstream buffer, causing a "Cannot write to finalized buffer" logical error

When an HTTP request with compression (LZ4/gzip) encounters an error, the error handling path in `cancelWithException` finalizes the HTTP response buffer (`out_holder`) after sending the error response. However, the intermediate compression buffer (`wrap_compressed_holder`) is left un-finalized. When `Output::cancel` is subsequently called, it invokes `cancel` on the compression buffer, whose `WriteBufferDecorator::cancelImpl` only checked `!out->isCanceled()` before attempting to flush remaining data to the downstream buffer. Since the downstream buffer was finalized (not canceled), the flush triggered "Cannot write to finalized buffer" — a logical error that aborts the server in debug builds.

The fix adds `&& !out->isFinalized()` to the validity check, and restructures the code to skip `this->next()` and `out->next()` calls when the downstream buffer is not writable.

Found via SQLancer testing (PR #98390).

### Changelog category (leave one):
- CI Fix or Improvement

### Changelog entry (a [user-readable short description](https://github.com/ClickHouse/ClickHouse/blob/master/docs/changelog_entry_guidelines.md) of the changes that goes into CHANGELOG.md):
Fix exception "Cannot write to finalized buffer" in `WriteBufferDecorator::cancelImpl` when HTTP requests with compression encounter errors.

### Documentation entry for user-facing changes

- [ ] Documentation is written (mandatory for new features)
author: alexey-milovidov

reviews.body: Ok.

commits.message:
Fix `WriteBufferDecorator::cancelImpl` writing to finalized buffer

In `cancelImpl`, the code only checked `!out->isCanceled()` before
attempting to flush data to the downstream buffer. However, the
downstream buffer could also be already finalized (not just canceled).

This happens in the HTTP handler error path: when `cancelWithException`
sends an error response to the client, it finalizes the HTTP response
buffer (`out_holder`), but the intermediate compression buffer
(`wrap_compressed_holder`) is left un-finalized. When `Output::cancel`
is then called, the compression buffer's `cancelImpl` tries to flush
its remaining data to the already-finalized downstream buffer, triggering
a "Cannot write to finalized buffer" exception (which is a logical error
that aborts the server in debug builds).

The fix adds `&& !out->isFinalized()` to the validity check, and also
restructures the code to skip `this->next()` and `out->next()` calls
when the downstream buffer is not writable.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

Add regression test for WriteBufferDecorator cancel with compressed error responses

Test sends queries that fail during analysis via HTTP with various
compression encodings (lz4, gzip, zstd, br, deflate) and verifies the
server returns an error without crashing.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

title: Fix progress stats for async insert queries
body:
Since `async_insert` defaults to `true`, INSERT queries go through the async insert path where the actual flush happens in a background thread with a separate query context. The original client query's `ProcessListElement` was never updated with write statistics, causing `written_rows`, `read_rows`, `result_rows` (and their byte counterparts) to be zeros in both the client output and `query_log`.

The fix communicates per-entry written rows/bytes from the background flush thread back to the waiting client via the `Entry` future (changed from `std::future<void>` to `std::future<WriteResult>`). After the future resolves, both the TCP and HTTP paths update the original query's `ProcessListElement` progress, which feeds into `query_log` and the client progress protocol.

Closes #99758

### Changelog category (leave one):
- Bug Fix (user-visible misbehavior in an official stable release)

### Changelog entry (a [user-readable short description](https://github.com/ClickHouse/ClickHouse/blob/master/docs/changelog_entry_guidelines.md) of the changes that goes into CHANGELOG.md):
Fix async insert queries reporting zero `written_rows`, `read_rows`, and `result_rows` in `query_log` and client output.

### Documentation entry for user-facing changes

- [ ] Documentation is written (mandatory for new features)
author: CheSema

commits.message:
fix: report progress stats for async insert queries

Since `async_insert` defaults to true, INSERT queries go through the
async insert path where the actual flush happens in a background thread
with a separate query context. The original client query's
`ProcessListElement` was never updated with write statistics, causing
`written_rows`, `read_rows`, `result_rows` (and their byte counterparts)
to be zeros in both the client output and `query_log`.

The fix communicates per-entry written rows/bytes from the background
flush thread back to the waiting client via the `Entry` future (changed
from `std::future<void>` to `std::future<WriteResult>`). After the
future resolves, both the TCP and HTTP paths update the original query's
`ProcessListElement` progress, which feeds into `query_log` and the
client progress protocol.

Closes https://github.com/ClickHouse/ClickHouse/issues/99758

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix: include result_rows in async insert progress sent to client

The clickhouse-client displays "N rows in set" from the result_rows
field of the Progress packet. Without this, the client would still show
"0 rows in set" even though written_rows was correct.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
assert that tcp and http provide result progress

fix(test): flush distributed buffer before drop in test_old_versions

The function-scoped `setup_nodes` fixture drops and recreates tables
between test iterations. Without flushing first, the `Distributed`
engine buffer on `node_oldest` may still have pending sends that
arrive after the table is recreated in the next iteration, causing
row count to accumulate across retries.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix: replace defensive branch with chassert in async insert finish

Unfinished entries always have a corresponding WriteResult in the map,
since entries with parse errors are finished immediately. Replace the
unreachable fallback with a chassert to catch invariant violations in
debug builds.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix: resolve build errors in async insert progress

`WriteResult` was an undeclared identifier — replaced with `ResultProgress`.
`ReadProgress` inside `WaitForAsyncInsertSource` resolved to
`IProcessor::ReadProgress` instead of `DB::ReadProgress` — added
explicit qualification.

https://s3.amazonaws.com/clickhouse-test-reports/json.html?PR=99879&sha=b7038e1c8760b7191cf448d78bfeb1570c4f3cbd&name_0=PR

title: Cherry pick #100902 to 26.3: Evaluate engine arguments for StorageAlias before storing the definition
body:

author: alexey-milovidov

commits.message:
Fix inconsistent AST formatting in `INTERPOLATE` with aliased expressions

`ASTInterpolateElement::formatImpl` produced unparseable output when
the interpolation expression carried an alias: `col AS expr AS alias`
(double `AS`).  The parser only accepts `col AS (expr AS alias)` with
explicit parentheses around the aliased sub-expression.

Also switch the column name output from raw `ostr << column` to
`settings.writeIdentifier` so identifiers are quoted consistently.

https://s3.amazonaws.com/clickhouse-test-reports/json.html?PR=98146&sha=0b4d3825421f0461f2c03714ffba01070f7dc717&name_0=PR&name_1=BuzzHouse%20%28amd_debug%29

title: Cherry pick #98441 to 26.2: Fix WriteBufferDecorator::cancelImpl writing to finalized buffer
body:
Original pull-request https://github.com/ClickHouse/ClickHouse/pull/98441

## Do not merge this PR manually

This pull-request is a first step of an automated backporting.
It contains changes similar to calling `git cherry-pick` locally.
If you intend to continue backporting the changes, then resolve all conflicts if any.
Otherwise, if you do not want to backport them, then just close this pull-request.

The check results does not matter at this step - you can safely ignore them.

### Troubleshooting

#### If the conflicts were resolved in a wrong way

If this cherry-pick PR is completely screwed by a wrong conflicts resolution, and you want to recreate it:

- delete the `pr-cherrypick` label from the PR
- delete this branch from the repository

You also need to check the **Original pull-request** for `pr-backports-created` label, and  delete if it's presented there


### The PR source
The PR is created in the [CI job](https://github.com/ClickHouse/ClickHouse/actions/runs/23753089437/job/69200326393)
author: robot-clickhouse-ci-2

commits.message:
Fix `WriteBufferDecorator::cancelImpl` writing to finalized buffer

In `cancelImpl`, the code only checked `!out->isCanceled()` before
attempting to flush data to the downstream buffer. However, the
downstream buffer could also be already finalized (not just canceled).

This happens in the HTTP handler error path: when `cancelWithException`
sends an error response to the client, it finalizes the HTTP response
buffer (`out_holder`), but the intermediate compression buffer
(`wrap_compressed_holder`) is left un-finalized. When `Output::cancel`
is then called, the compression buffer's `cancelImpl` tries to flush
its remaining data to the already-finalized downstream buffer, triggering
a "Cannot write to finalized buffer" exception (which is a logical error
that aborts the server in debug builds).

The fix adds `&& !out->isFinalized()` to the validity check, and also
restructures the code to skip `this->next()` and `out->next()` calls
when the downstream buffer is not writable.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

Add regression test for WriteBufferDecorator cancel with compressed error responses

Test sends queries that fail during analysis via HTTP with various
compression encodings (lz4, gzip, zstd, br, deflate) and verifies the
server returns an error without crashing.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

Merge pull request #98441 from ClickHouse/fix-write-buffer-decorator-cancel

Fix WriteBufferDecorator::cancelImpl writing to finalized buffer

title: Fix LOGICAL_ERROR due to patch parts column order mismatch
body:
<!-- Fixes issue link part, do not remove -->
## Linked issues
- fixes https://github.com/ClickHouse/clickhouse-core-incidents/issues/1021
<!-- End of fixes issue link part, do not remove -->

## Issue
In `addPatchPartsColumns`, patch column names are collected into a `NameSet` (std::unordered_set) and then converted to a Names vector. The iteration order of std::unordered_set is non-deterministic and depends on the standard library's hash table implementation. When two patch parts have different system column sets (Merge mode uses `{_part, _part_offset, _data_version}`, Join mode uses `{_block_number, _block_offset, _data_version})`, the different hash table layouts can cause user columns to iterate in different orders. `getUpdatedHeader` then compares patch headers positionally via `assertCompatibleHeader`, which throws LOGICAL_ERROR on column name mismatch.

## The fix
Sort columns via `header.sortColumns()` in `getUpdatedHeader` before the cross-patch assertCompatibleHeader comparison — a defensive second layer, since the downstream consumers (`createPatchForColumn`, `applyPatchesToBlockRaw`) use name-based lookups and are unaffected by column order.

## The test
Add a `patch_parts_reverse_column_order` failpoint that deliberately reverses column order for odd-indexed patches, making the bug reproducible regardless of the standard library's hash table behavior. The regression test creates a scenario with both Merge and Join mode patches and verifies correct query results.

### Changelog category (leave one):
- Bug Fix (user-visible misbehavior in an official stable release)

### Changelog entry (a [user-readable short description](https://github.com/ClickHouse/ClickHouse/blob/master/docs/changelog_entry_guidelines.md) of the changes that goes into CHANGELOG.md):
Fix LOGICAL_ERROR due to patch parts column order mismatch

### Documentation entry for user-facing changes

- [ ] Documentation is written (mandatory for new features)

<!---
Directly edit documentation source files in the "docs" folder with the same pull-request as code changes

or

Add a user-readable short description of the changes that should be added to docs.clickhouse.com below.

At a minimum, the following information should be added (but add more as needed).
- Motivation: Why is this function, table engine, etc. useful to ClickHouse users?

- Parameters: If the feature being added takes arguments, options or is influenced by settings, please list them below with a brief explanation.

- Example use: A query or command.
-->

<!-- CURSOR_SUMMARY -->
---

> [!NOTE]
> **Medium Risk**
> Touches MergeTree patch-part read/apply paths; while intended to be order-only, it affects how patch blocks are constructed/compared and could impact lightweight update/query behavior if assumptions change.
> 
> **Overview**
> Fixes a `LOGICAL_ERROR` when applying multiple patch parts by making patch-block column ordering deterministic.
> 
> Patch-part column lists are now sorted before reading, and `getUpdatedHeader` defensively sorts filtered patch headers prior to cross-patch `assertCompatibleHeader` checks. A new `patch_parts_reverse_column_order` failpoint and stateless regression test reproduce the prior mismatch between Merge- and Join-mode patches and verify stable results.
> 
> <sup>Written by [Cursor Bugbot](https://cursor.com/dashboard?tab=bugbot) for commit 232c29bfeadedf927b1a1cc8d44cf157ad4ad060. This will update automatically on new commits. Configure [here](https://cursor.com/dashboard?tab=bugbot).</sup>
<!-- /CURSOR_SUMMARY -->
author: pamarcos

reviews.body:
## Pull request overview

This PR fixes a LOGICAL_ERROR triggered when applying multiple patch parts that have the same updated columns but arrive with different column orders. It makes patch block/header ordering deterministic (normalized by column name) and adds a failpoint-driven regression test to ensure the mismatch is caught.

**Changes:**
- Normalize patch headers in `getUpdatedHeader()` by sorting columns by name before cross-patch compatibility checks.
- Make patch-part column reads deterministic by sorting requested column names (and add a failpoint to reverse order for selected patches in tests).
- Add a stateless regression test reproducing issue #1021 and validating correct behavior after merges/updates.

### Reviewed changes

Copilot reviewed 5 out of 5 changed files in this pull request and generated no comments.

<details>
<summary>Show a summary per file</summary>

| File | Description |
| ---- | ----------- |
| tests/queries/0_stateless/04034_patch_parts_column_order_mismatch.sql | New regression test that enables a failpoint to force differing patch column orders and validates patch application across Merge/Join modes. |
| tests/queries/0_stateless/04034_patch_parts_column_order_mismatch.reference | Expected output for the new regression test. |
| src/Storages/MergeTree/PatchParts/applyPatches.cpp | Sorts the updated header’s columns by name to avoid positional header mismatches across patches. |
| src/Storages/MergeTree/MergeTreeBlockReadUtils.cpp | Sorts patch column name list to ensure deterministic patch block column order; adds failpoint hook to reverse order for odd-indexed patches. |
| src/Common/FailPoint.cpp | Registers the new `patch_parts_reverse_column_order` failpoint. |
</details>

## Pull request overview

Copilot reviewed 5 out of 5 changed files in this pull request and generated 1 comment.
LGTM

commits.message:
Fix LOGICAL_ERROR exception in `assertCompatibleHeader` when reading patch parts

When multiple patch parts (Merge and Join mode) update the same columns,
`addPatchPartsColumns` collects column names into a `NameSet`
(`std::unordered_set`) and then converts them to a `Names` vector.
The iteration order of `std::unordered_set` is non-deterministic, so
different patch parts reading the same user columns can produce blocks
with different column orderings. This causes a LOGICAL_ERROR exception
in `getUpdatedHeader` -> `assertCompatibleHeader` which compares
patch headers positionally:

  Code: 49. DB::Exception: Block structure mismatch in patch parts
  stream: different names of columns

Fix: sort the column names after extracting them from the `NameSet` to
ensure all patch parts produce blocks with the same deterministic column
order.

Add defensive sort in getUpdatedHeader, debug-build reverse, and simplify test

- Sort columns in getUpdatedHeader via header.sortColumns() before
  cross-patch assertCompatibleHeader comparison. This is a defensive
  second layer: even if addPatchPartsColumns produces different column
  orderings, getUpdatedHeader normalizes them before comparing.

- In debug/sanitizer builds, deliberately reverse column order for
  odd-indexed patches in addPatchPartsColumns. This deterministically
  triggers the LOGICAL_ERROR without the getUpdatedHeader sort fix,
  ensuring the regression test catches the bug.

- Simplify the test to use plain MergeTree (no ZooKeeper dependency)
  and remove unnecessary tags/settings.

Replace DEBUG_OR_SANITIZER_BUILD reverse with a failpoint

Replace the #ifdef DEBUG_OR_SANITIZER_BUILD block that reversed column
order for odd-indexed patches with a proper failpoint
`patch_parts_reverse_column_order`. This follows the established pattern
for fault injection in the codebase (libfiu + SYSTEM ENABLE FAILPOINT).

The test now explicitly enables/disables the failpoint via SQL, making it
work in any build configuration where libfiu is available.
fixup! Replace DEBUG_OR_SANITIZER_BUILD reverse with a failpoint

Remove redundant sort in addPatchPartsColumns, improve comments

The sort in addPatchPartsColumns is unnecessary: the actual fix is
sortColumns() in getUpdatedHeader which normalizes column order before
the positional assertCompatibleHeader comparison. The failpoint in
addPatchPartsColumns simulates the non-deterministic NameSet iteration
that causes the bug, and should not be preceded by a sort that masks it.

If this cherry-pick PR is completely screwed by a wrong conflicts resolution, and you want to recreate it:

- delete the `pr-cherrypick` label from the PR
- delete this branch from the repository

You also need to check the **Original pull-request** for `pr-backports-created` label, and  delete if it's presented there


### The PR source
The PR is created in the [CI job](https://github.com/ClickHouse/ClickHouse/actions/runs/23747555383/job/69180194986)
author: robot-clickhouse-ci-1

commits.message:
fix: report progress stats for async insert queries

Since `async_insert` defaults to true, INSERT queries go through the
async insert path where the actual flush happens in a background thread
with a separate query context. The original client query's
`ProcessListElement` was never updated with write statistics, causing
`written_rows`, `read_rows`, `result_rows` (and their byte counterparts)
to be zeros in both the client output and `query_log`.

The fix communicates per-entry written rows/bytes from the background
flush thread back to the waiting client via the `Entry` future (changed
from `std::future<void>` to `std::future<WriteResult>`). After the
future resolves, both the TCP and HTTP paths update the original query's
`ProcessListElement` progress, which feeds into `query_log` and the
client progress protocol.

Closes https://github.com/ClickHouse/ClickHouse/issues/99758

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix: include result_rows in async insert progress sent to client

The clickhouse-client displays "N rows in set" from the result_rows
field of the Progress packet. Without this, the client would still show
"0 rows in set" even though written_rows was correct.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
assert that tcp and http provide result progress

fix(test): flush distributed buffer before drop in test_old_versions

The function-scoped `setup_nodes` fixture drops and recreates tables
between test iterations. Without flushing first, the `Distributed`
engine buffer on `node_oldest` may still have pending sends that
arrive after the table is recreated in the next iteration, causing
row count to accumulate across retries.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix: replace defensive branch with chassert in async insert finish

Unfinished entries always have a corresponding WriteResult in the map,
since entries with parse errors are finished immediately. Replace the
unreachable fallback with a chassert to catch invariant violations in
debug builds.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix: resolve build errors in async insert progress

`WriteResult` was an undeclared identifier — replaced with `ResultProgress`.
`ReadProgress` inside `WaitForAsyncInsertSource` resolved to
`IProcessor::ReadProgress` instead of `DB::ReadProgress` — added
explicit qualification.

https://s3.amazonaws.com/clickhouse-test-reports/json.html?PR=99879&sha=b7038e1c8760b7191cf448d78bfeb1570c4f3cbd&name_0=PR

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

Merge pull request #99879 from ClickHouse/fix/async-insert-progress

Fix progress stats for async insert queries

title: Fix N+1 `HeadObject` calls for S3 brace-expansion globs
body:
### Changelog category (leave one):
- Bug Fix (user-visible misbehavior in an official stable release)


### Changelog entry (a [user-readable short description](https://github.com/ClickHouse/ClickHouse/blob/master/docs/changelog_entry_guidelines.md) of the changes that goes into CHANGELOG.md):
Fix N+1 `HeadObject` calls for S3 brace-expansion globs.
author: thevar1able

reviews.body:
<!-- BUGBOT_REVIEW -->
Cursor Bugbot has reviewed your changes and found 1 potential issue.



<!-- BUGBOT_AUTOFIX_REVIEW_FOOTNOTE_BEGIN -->
<sup>Bugbot Autofix is OFF. To automatically fix reported issues with cloud agents, have a team admin enable autofix in the [Cursor dashboard](https://www.cursor.com/dashboard?tab=bugbot).</sup>
<!-- BUGBOT_AUTOFIX_REVIEW_FOOTNOTE_END -->
Overall it LGTM, just one comment on the test.

commits.message:
Fix N+1 HeadObject calls for S3 brace-expansion globs

When reading from S3 with brace-expansion globs like `{a,b,c}.tsv`,
`getPathSample` used to create a file iterator just to obtain a sample
path string for hive partitioning. This caused an extra HeadObject call
(N+1 instead of N for N files).

Since `getPathSample` only needs a path string (not S3 metadata), for
pure brace-expansion globs we can use `expandSelectionGlob` to expand
the glob locally and return the first expanded path directly — zero S3
API calls. The file iterator fallback is preserved for wildcard globs
(`*`, `?`) and range globs (`{1..3}`) which require ListObjects.

Assisted-by: Claude Opus 4.6 via GitHub Copilot

Add missing `isArchive` guard to `containsOnlyEnumGlobs` shortcut in `getPathSample`

The `containsOnlyEnumGlobs` check lacked the `!configuration->isArchive()`
guard that the first early-return already had. Since `containsOnlyEnumGlobs`
returns true for strings with no glob characters at all, archive paths would
incorrectly hit this branch and return the raw archive path instead of falling
through to the file iterator which returns paths from inside the archive.

Assisted-by: Claude Opus 4.6 via GitHub Copilot

Use local_distributed_processing in non-glob fast path of getPathSample

Assisted-by: Claude Opus 4.6 via GitHub Copilot

Add no-parallel-replicas tag to S3 brace glob HeadObject test

Assisted-by: Claude Opus 4.6 via GitHub Copilot

title: Fix interpolate: disambiguate interpolated columns with __interpolate function
body:
### Changelog category (leave one):
- Bug Fix (user-visible misbehavior in an official stable release)


### Changelog entry (a [user-readable short description](https://github.com/ClickHouse/ClickHouse/blob/master/docs/changelog_entry_guidelines.md) of the changes that goes into CHANGELOG.md):
Prevent different interpolated columns from collapse into the same column in a block when interpolated columns are effectively aliases of the same column


closes #71858
closes [#381](https://github.com/ClickHouse/clickhouse-core-incidents/issues/381)

The source of the bug is that interpolated columns can be just aliases of another column and as a result they are resolved into that single column and during interpolation transform, rows for different interpolated columns are written into the same resulting column. Solved by introduction of internal `__interpolate()` function which just returns its first argument (original column), but also accepts second argument (which is an alias of an interpolated column) which creates unique column (by introducing unique function's signature) disambiguating it from original.
author: yakov-olkhovskiy

commits.message: add __interpolate function and replace interpolated collumns with it
allow __interpolate to have no documentation and no version info - it's an exclusively internal function
do not wrap in __interpolate second time (on remote)

title: Fix flaky test 00163_shard_join_with_empty_table
body:
Pin `max_block_size = 65536` to prevent non-deterministic output when `LIMIT` is applied to a distributed stream from `system.numbers`.

With randomized `max_block_size` (e.g. 78753), the first query's `LIMIT 10` can select rows starting from the block-size offset (78753) instead of 0, producing wrong output. The exact mechanism: with `max_block_size=78753`, each shard sends blocks of that size, and under CI parallel load conditions, the block delivery order causes `LIMIT` to skip the first block's rows entirely.

CI evidence (PR #97813): output was `[78753, 78754, ..., 78762]` instead of `[0, 1, ..., 9]` — offset matches `max_block_size` exactly.

This revision preserves infinite stream semantics (`system.numbers` kept) per reviewer feedback.

### Changelog category (leave one):
- CI Fix or Improvement (changelog entry is not required)

### Changelog entry (a user-readable short description of the changes that goes into CHANGELOG.md):
...

### Documentation entry for user-facing changes

- [ ] Documentation is written (mandatory for new features)

commits.message:
Fix flaky test 00163_shard_join_with_empty_table

Pin max_block_size=65536 to prevent non-deterministic output when LIMIT
is applied to a distributed stream from system.numbers. With randomized
max_block_size (e.g. 78753), rows can be selected starting from the
block-size offset instead of 0, depending on block delivery order from
remote shards.

Preserves infinite stream semantics as intended by the test design.

title: Fix SIGABRT crash when aggregate_functions_null_for_empty is used with Null combinator functions
body:
### Changelog category (leave one):
- Bug Fix (user-visible misbehavior in an official stable release)

### Changelog entry (a user-readable short description of the changes that goes to CHANGELOG.md):

Fix server crash (SIGABRT) when using aggregate functions with the internal-only `Null` combinator (e.g. `sumNull`, `avgNull`) and `aggregate_functions_null_for_empty = 1` setting enabled.

### What this PR fixes

In `rewriteAggregateFunctionNameIfNeeded()` (QueryAnalyzer.cpp:950), the code calls `tryGetProperties()` and immediately dereferences the returned `std::optional` without checking if it has a value:

```cpp
auto properties = AggregateFunctionFactory::instance().tryGetProperties(result_aggregate_function_name, action);
if (!properties->returns_default_when_only_null)  // ← crash when properties is nullopt
```

`tryGetProperties()` returns `nullopt` for functions using the internal-only `Null` combinator. The `Null` combinator passes `isAggregateFunctionName()` (which doesn't check `isForInternalUsageOnly()`) but fails `tryGetProperties()` (which does check). When `aggregate_functions_null_for_empty = 1`, this path is entered and the dereference of an empty optional calls `std::abort()` → SIGABRT (signal 6).

The correct pattern is already used 12 lines below (line 962) for the same `tryGetProperties()` call:
```cpp
if (function_properies && !function_properies->returns_default_when_only_null)
```

### Reproduction

```sql
SELECT sumNull(number) FROM numbers(10) SETTINGS aggregate_functions_null_for_empty = 1;
-- Server crashes with signal 6 (SIGABRT)
```

After fix: returns error Code 63 `UNKNOWN_AGGREGATE_FUNCTION` ("Aggregate function combinator 'Null' is only for internal usage").

### CI impact

This crash was hitting BuzzHouse CI on master and 12+ unrelated PRs across multiple build types (amd_tsan, arm_asan_ubsan, amd_debug, amd_msan) since 2026-03-22:
- **STID:** 5390-4fca, 5390-57f3
- **14 failures** in 8 days, accelerating

### Test

Added regression test `04068_aggregate_null_for_empty_with_null_combinator` that verifies the server returns a proper error instead of crashing. Passes 50/50 with full randomization.
author: groeneai

commits.message:
Fix SIGABRT crash in rewriteAggregateFunctionNameIfNeeded

When `aggregate_functions_null_for_empty` is enabled, the function
`rewriteAggregateFunctionNameIfNeeded` calls `tryGetProperties()` and
dereferences the result without checking if the optional has a value.
`tryGetProperties()` returns nullopt for aggregate functions using the
internal-only `Null` combinator (e.g. `sumNull`, `avgNull`), causing
`std::optional::operator->` to abort.

The fix adds a null check before dereferencing, matching the pattern
already used 12 lines below at line 962 for the same function.

This crash was hitting BuzzHouse CI on master and 12+ unrelated PRs
(STID: 5390-4fca, 5390-57f3) since 2026-03-22.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

title: Add highlight() function for search term highlighting with tag wrapping
body: ### Changelog category (leave one): - New Feature ### Changelog entry (a user-readable short description of the changes that goes into CHANGELOG.md): Add `highlight()` function that wraps occurrences of search terms in a text string with HTML tags (default ``/``). Supports ASCII case-insensitive matching, automatic merging of overlapping matches, and custom open/close tags. ### Documentation entry for user-facing changes - [x] Documentation is written (mandatory for new features) **Syntax**: `highlight(haystack, needles[, open_tag, close_tag])` - `haystack` — input text (`String` or `FixedString`) - `needles` — constant `Array(String)` of search terms (max 255) - `open_tag` / `close_tag` — optional custom tags (default: `` / ``) #96803 **Example**: ```sql SELECT highlight('The quick brown fox', ['quick', 'fox']); -- Result: The quick brown fox SELECT highlight('Hello World', ['hello'], '', ''); -- Result: Hello World Matching is ASCII case-insensitive. Overlapping or adjacent matches are merged into a single highlighted span. --- > [!NOTE] > **Medium Risk** > Adds a new SQL function that performs multi-needle matching and rewrites output strings, plus a new setting to cap per-row matches; risk is mainly around performance/memory behavior and edge cases in string handling (e.g., FixedString conversion, overlapping matches). > > **Overview** > Adds a new `highlight(haystack, needles[, open_tag, close_tag])` SQL function that performs ASCII case-insensitive multi-term matching, merges overlapping/adjacent matches, and returns the input text with matched regions wrapped in tags (default `''`/`''`), including support for `FixedString` inputs. > > Introduces `highlight_max_matches_per_row` (default `10000`) to prevent excessive per-row matches from causing high memory usage, throwing `LIMIT_EXCEEDED` when exceeded, and adds unit, stateless query, and performance tests covering basic behavior, overlap handling, UTF-8 non-ASCII case behavior, and error cases (empty needles / >255 needles). > > Written by [Cursor Bugbot](https://cursor.com/dashboard?tab=bugbot) for commit 6cf9ddcdab4a6adb8fe5b73eb237d0f8758933e2. This will update automatically on new commits. Configure [here](https://cursor.com/dashboard?tab=bugbot).
author: fastio

title: Cherry pick #99879 to 26.3: Fix progress stats for async insert queries
body:
Original pull-request https://github.com/ClickHouse/ClickHouse/pull/99879

## Do not merge this PR manually

This pull-request is a first step of an automated backporting.
It contains changes similar to calling `git cherry-pick` locally.
If you intend to continue backporting the changes, then resolve all conflicts if any.
Otherwise, if you do not want to backport them, then just close this pull-request.

The check results does not matter at this step - you can safely ignore them.

### Troubleshooting

#### If the conflicts were resolved in a wrong way

If this cherry-pick PR is completely screwed by a wrong conflicts resolution, and you want to recreate it:

- delete the `pr-cherrypick` label from the PR
- delete this branch from the repository

You also need to check the **Original pull-request** for `pr-backports-created` label, and  delete if it's presented there


### The PR source
The PR is created in the [CI job](https://github.com/ClickHouse/ClickHouse/actions/runs/23747555383/job/69180194986)
author: robot-clickhouse-ci-1

commits.message:
fix: report progress stats for async insert queries

Since `async_insert` defaults to true, INSERT queries go through the
async insert path where the actual flush happens in a background thread
with a separate query context. The original client query's
`ProcessListElement` was never updated with write statistics, causing
`written_rows`, `read_rows`, `result_rows` (and their byte counterparts)
to be zeros in both the client output and `query_log`.

The fix communicates per-entry written rows/bytes from the background
flush thread back to the waiting client via the `Entry` future (changed
from `std::future<void>` to `std::future<WriteResult>`). After the
future resolves, both the TCP and HTTP paths update the original query's
`ProcessListElement` progress, which feeds into `query_log` and the
client progress protocol.

Closes https://github.com/ClickHouse/ClickHouse/issues/99758

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix: include result_rows in async insert progress sent to client

The clickhouse-client displays "N rows in set" from the result_rows
field of the Progress packet. Without this, the client would still show
"0 rows in set" even though written_rows was correct.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
assert that tcp and http provide result progress

fix(test): flush distributed buffer before drop in test_old_versions

The function-scoped `setup_nodes` fixture drops and recreates tables
between test iterations. Without flushing first, the `Distributed`
engine buffer on `node_oldest` may still have pending sends that
arrive after the table is recreated in the next iteration, causing
row count to accumulate across retries.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix: replace defensive branch with chassert in async insert finish

Unfinished entries always have a corresponding WriteResult in the map,
since entries with parse errors are finished immediately. Replace the
unreachable fallback with a chassert to catch invariant violations in
debug builds.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix: resolve build errors in async insert progress

`WriteResult` was an undeclared identifier — replaced with `ResultProgress`.
`ReadProgress` inside `WaitForAsyncInsertSource` resolved to
`IProcessor::ReadProgress` instead of `DB::ReadProgress` — added
explicit qualification.

https://s3.amazonaws.com/clickhouse-test-reports/json.html?PR=99879&sha=b7038e1c8760b7191cf448d78bfeb1570c4f3cbd&name_0=PR

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

Merge pull request #99879 from ClickHouse/fix/async-insert-progress

Fix progress stats for async insert queries

title: Fix DROP COLUMN + ADD COLUMN resurrecting stale data from unapplied mutations
body:
When a DROP COLUMN mutation has not yet been applied to a part, and the column is re-added with the same name, reading could return stale data from before the drop instead of the default value.

Root cause: `DROP_COLUMN` was not tracked as a metadata mutation in `AlterConversions`, so the reading code had no way to know that a pending DROP mutation existed for a column still present in the part.

The fix:
- Add `DROP_COLUMN` to `AlterConversions::isSupportedMetadataMutation` so that pending DROP mutations are included in the mutations snapshot.
- Track dropped column names in `AlterConversions::dropped_columns`.
- In the MergeTree readers (Wide and Compact), skip reading data for columns that were dropped by a pending mutation. The column is left as nullptr and filled with the default value by `fillMissingColumns`.
- In `injectRequiredColumnsRecursively`, treat dropped columns as missing so that columns required for default expressions are injected.

### Changelog category (leave one):
- Improvement


### Changelog entry (a [user-readable short description](https://github.com/ClickHouse/ClickHouse/blob/master/docs/changelog_entry_guidelines.md) of the changes that goes into CHANGELOG.md):
Prevent the resurrection of dropped data if ADD COLUMN is run quickly after the DROP COLUMN mutation.
author: alexey-milovidov

commits.message:
Fix DROP COLUMN + ADD COLUMN resurrecting stale data from unapplied mutations

When a DROP COLUMN mutation has not yet been applied to a part and
the column is re-added with the same name, reading could return stale
data from before the drop instead of the default value.

Root cause: `DROP_COLUMN` was not tracked as a metadata mutation in
`AlterConversions`, so the reading code had no way to know that a
pending DROP mutation existed for a column still present in the part.

The fix:
- Add `DROP_COLUMN` to `AlterConversions::isSupportedMetadataMutation`
  so that pending DROP mutations are included in the mutations snapshot.
- Track dropped column names in `AlterConversions::dropped_columns`.
- In the MergeTree readers (Wide and Compact), skip reading data for
  columns that were dropped by a pending mutation. The column is left
  as nullptr and filled with the default value by `fillMissingColumns`.
- In `injectRequiredColumnsRecursively`, treat dropped columns as
  missing so that columns required for default expressions are injected.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

title: Fix ALTER atomicity when combining UPDATE/DELETE with RENAME COLUMN
body:
Fixes #70678

When an ALTER statement combined UPDATE or DELETE with RENAME COLUMN on the same column, the RENAME would succeed even if the mutation validation failed, leaving the table in an inconsistent state.

For example:
```sql
CREATE TABLE t0 (c0 Int) ENGINE = Memory();
ALTER TABLE t0 UPDATE c0 = 1 WHERE TRUE, RENAME COLUMN c0 TO c1;
-- Error occurred, but column was renamed anyway!
INSERT INTO TABLE t0 (c0) VALUES (1);  -- Fails: No such column c0
```

Root cause: In `InterpreterAlterQuery::executeToTable`, alter commands (including RENAME) are executed BEFORE mutation commands (UPDATE/DELETE) are validated. If mutation validation fails after the rename is committed, the table schema is already changed.

The fix adds early validation that detects conflicts between RENAME COLUMN and UPDATE/DELETE operations before any changes are made:

1. If an UPDATE references a column that is being renamed, reject early
2. If a DELETE/UPDATE predicate references a column being renamed, reject early
3. Operations on different columns continue to work normally

Error messages are clear and actionable:
- "Cannot UPDATE column `c0` and RENAME it in the same ALTER query. Please split into separate ALTER statements."
- "Cannot use column `c0` in DELETE predicate and RENAME it in the same ALTER query. Please split into separate ALTER statements."

### Changelog category (leave one):
- Improvement


### Changelog entry (a [user-readable short description](https://github.com/ClickHouse/ClickHouse/blob/master/docs/changelog_entry_guidelines.md) of the changes that goes into CHANGELOG.md):
In previous versions, when combining conflicting ALTER expressions with UPDATE and RENAME COLUMN, a logical error was thrown instead of a proper exception. Closes #70678.
author: alexey-milovidov

reviews.body: Looks good.

commits.message:
Fix ALTER atomicity when combining UPDATE/DELETE with RENAME COLUMN

Fixes #70678

When an ALTER statement combined UPDATE or DELETE with RENAME COLUMN
on the same column, the RENAME would succeed even if the mutation
validation failed, leaving the table in an inconsistent state.

For example:
```sql
CREATE TABLE t0 (c0 Int) ENGINE = Memory();
ALTER TABLE t0 UPDATE c0 = 1 WHERE TRUE, RENAME COLUMN c0 TO c1;
-- Error occurred, but column was renamed anyway!
INSERT INTO TABLE t0 (c0) VALUES (1);  -- Fails: No such column c0
```

Root cause: In `InterpreterAlterQuery::executeToTable`, alter commands
(including RENAME) are executed BEFORE mutation commands (UPDATE/DELETE)
are validated. If mutation validation fails after the rename is committed,
the table schema is already changed.

The fix adds early validation that detects conflicts between RENAME COLUMN
and UPDATE/DELETE operations before any changes are made:

1. If an UPDATE references a column that is being renamed, reject early
2. If a DELETE/UPDATE predicate references a column being renamed, reject early
3. Operations on different columns continue to work normally

Error messages are clear and actionable:
- "Cannot UPDATE column `c0` and RENAME it in the same ALTER query.
   Please split into separate ALTER statements."
- "Cannot use column `c0` in DELETE predicate and RENAME it in the same
   ALTER query. Please split into separate ALTER statements."

title: Fix flaky test `03794_read_in_order_virtual_row_and_sparse_primary_key_msan`
body:
The `read_in_order_use_virtual_row` optimization creates a `virtual_row_conversion` expression that expects specific primary key columns as input. However, `optimizeIndexColumns` can drop suffix columns from the in-memory index when they have high cardinality (controlled by `primary_key_ratio_of_unique_prefix_values_to_skip_suffix_columns`).

The original code used `std::min()` to handle the mismatch, creating a `pk_block` with fewer columns than needed. This caused "Not found column in block" errors when `setVirtualRow` tried to execute the conversion.

The fix skips adding `VirtualRowTransform` for parts where the index doesn't have all required columns. This is safe because the virtual row optimization is optional - it improves performance but isn't required for correctness.

### Changelog category (leave one):
- CI Fix or Improvement (changelog entry is not required)

See https://s3.amazonaws.com/clickhouse-test-reports/json.html?PR=95078&sha=6650dbe45f3e88ceb03dccb65f8070edd0fcc390&name_0=PR&name_1=Stateless%20tests%20%28amd_tsan%2C%20s3%20storage%2C%20parallel%2C%201%2F2%29
author: alexey-milovidov

commits.message:
Fix flaky test 03794_read_in_order_virtual_row_and_sparse_primary_key_msan

The `read_in_order_use_virtual_row` optimization creates a
`virtual_row_conversion` expression that expects specific primary key
columns as input. However, `optimizeIndexColumns` can drop suffix columns
from the in-memory index when they have high cardinality (controlled by
`primary_key_ratio_of_unique_prefix_values_to_skip_suffix_columns`).

The original code used `std::min()` to handle the mismatch, creating a
`pk_block` with fewer columns than needed. This caused "Not found column
in block" errors when `setVirtualRow` tried to execute the conversion.

The fix skips adding `VirtualRowTransform` for parts where the index
doesn't have all required columns. This is safe because the virtual row
optimization is optional - it improves performance but isn't required
for correctness.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.5 <noreply@anthropic.com>

Add test for virtual row optimization with sparse primary key index

This test verifies that the `read_in_order_use_virtual_row` optimization
works correctly when the in-memory primary key index has fewer columns
than the full primary key due to `optimizeIndexColumns` dropping suffix
columns with high cardinality.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.5 <noreply@anthropic.com>

title: Fix flaky tests 03632_skip_indices_logged and 04039_skip_index_query_log
body:
### Changelog category (leave one):
- CI Fix or Improvement (changelog entry is not required)

### Changelog entry (a user-readable short description of the changes that goes to CHANGELOG.md):
- none

### Description

Fixes flaky tests `03632_skip_indices_logged_in_query_log` and `04039_skip_index_query_log_actually_applied` which verify that `query_log.skip_indices` is populated correctly.

**Two root causes:**

1. **`auto_statistics_types=minmax` (randomized MergeTree setting)** creates MinMax statistics that perform *part-level pruning* before skip indices can perform *granule-level filtering*. When statistics prune all parts, skip indices see 0 granules, drop 0, and `skip_indices` stays empty.

   **Fix:** Pin `auto_statistics_types = ''` in `CREATE TABLE SETTINGS` for all tables in both tests.

2. **`use_skip_indexes_on_data_read=1` in test 03632** evaluates skip indices during data reading, but `query_log.skip_indices` only tracks indices evaluated during mark selection (`use_skip_indexes_on_data_read=0`).

   **Fix:** Change to `use_skip_indexes_on_data_read=0` in test 03632 (test 04039 already used `=0`). Update the `.reference` file accordingly.

**Reproduction (root cause 1):**
```bash
clickhouse client --allow_merge_tree_settings --auto_statistics_types minmax -q "
  CREATE TABLE t (a UInt64, INDEX idx_a a TYPE minmax GRANULARITY 1)
  ENGINE = MergeTree ORDER BY tuple() SETTINGS index_granularity = 8192;
  -- ... skip indices will be empty in query_log
"
```

**Reproduction (root cause 2):**
```bash
-- use_skip_indexes_on_data_read=1 → skip_indices empty
-- use_skip_indexes_on_data_read=0 → skip_indices populated
```

**Verified:** 50/50 passes on both tests with full randomization (query + MergeTree settings).

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
author: groeneai

commits.message:
Fix flaky tests 03632_skip_indices_logged and 04039_skip_index_query_log

Two root causes for skip_indices being empty in query_log:

1. auto_statistics_types=minmax (randomized MergeTree setting) creates
   MinMax statistics that prune parts BEFORE skip indices evaluate.
   When statistics prune all parts, skip indices see 0 granules, drop 0,
   and nothing gets logged. Fix: pin auto_statistics_types='' in
   CREATE TABLE SETTINGS for all three tables.

2. use_skip_indexes_on_data_read=1 evaluates skip indices during data
   reading, but query_log.skip_indices only tracks indices evaluated
   during mark selection. Fix: change to use_skip_indexes_on_data_read=0
   in test 03632 (test 04039 already used =0).

title: Fix inconsistent casting of Bool type in IN operator
body:
```sql
SELECT CAST(1, 'Bool') IN (10);
0
SELECT CAST(1, 'Bool') IN (255);
0
SELECT CAST(1, 'Bool') IN (256);
1 (should be 0)
SELECT CAST(1, 'Bool') IN (10000);
1 (should be 0)
```

### Changelog category (leave one):
- Backward Incompatible Change

### Changelog entry (a user-readable short description of the changes that goes to CHANGELOG.md):
The `IN` operator now uses exact value semantics for `Bool` type: only `0` and `1` values in the set match `Bool` values. Previously, numeric values greater than `255` in the `IN` set were incorrectly clamped to true when compared against `Bool`, so `SELECT CAST(1, 'Bool') IN (256)` returned 1. Now it correctly returns `0`. Closes https://github.com/ClickHouse/ClickHouse/issues/92980.
author: ashrithb

reviews.body: Thanks for working on the issue! Please add a test.

commits.message:
Fix inconsistent casting of Bool type in IN operator (#92980)

The Bool type in ClickHouse is implemented as UInt8 with a custom name.
When converting values for the IN operator, the equals() check between
UInt8 and Bool types returns true (same underlying typeid), causing
values like 10 or 255 to be stored as-is instead of being converted
to 0/1 (false/true).

This fix adds a special case in both convertFieldToType() and
convertFieldToTypeImpl() to detect when the target type is Bool
but the source type is not Bool (e.g., plain UInt8), and proceed
with proper Bool conversion instead of short-circuiting.

Before fix:
  SELECT CAST(1, 'Bool') IN (10);   -- returned 0 (incorrect)
  SELECT CAST(1, 'Bool') IN (255);  -- returned 0 (incorrect)
  SELECT CAST(1, 'Bool') IN (256);  -- returned 1 (correct)
  SELECT CAST(1, 'Bool') IN (10000);-- returned 1 (correct)

After fix:
  All non-zero values correctly convert to Bool true (1)
  Zero correctly converts to Bool false (0)

Add test for Bool IN operator casting fix (#92980)

title: Fix race between `setDeduplicationWindowSize` and `shutdown` in `MergeTreeDeduplicationLog`
body:
`setDeduplicationWindowSize` did not check the `stopped` flag, so a concurrent `ALTER TABLE MODIFY SETTING non_replicated_deduplication_window` could create a new `current_writer` after `shutdown` had already run. When the table was later destroyed, the destructor found `stopped == true` and returned early, leaving the new writer neither finalized nor canceled, triggering the "WriteBuffer is neither finalized nor canceled in destructor" assertion.

Add the `stopped` check, consistent with `addPart` and `dropPart`.

https://s3.amazonaws.com/clickhouse-test-reports/json.html?REF=master&sha=dba26e75cf6387d544694fc41350e013797b42ac&name_0=MasterCI&name_1=BuzzHouse%20%28amd_msan%29

- CI Fix or Improvement (changelog entry is not required)

commits.message:
Fix race between `setDeduplicationWindowSize` and `shutdown` in `MergeTreeDeduplicationLog`

`setDeduplicationWindowSize` did not check the `stopped` flag, so a
concurrent `ALTER TABLE MODIFY SETTING non_replicated_deduplication_window`
could create a new `current_writer` after `shutdown` had already run.
When the table was later destroyed, the destructor found `stopped == true`
and returned early, leaving the new writer neither finalized nor canceled,
triggering the "WriteBuffer is neither finalized nor canceled in destructor"
assertion.

Add the `stopped` check, consistent with `addPart` and `dropPart`.

title: Fix two latent defects: nullable flag in parallel merge and FixedString in JIT getColumnData
body:
Two latent defects found during code review of #100687 and #100577. Neither is currently triggerable — both are defensive fixes for consistency and correctness.

**1. Missing nullable flag propagation in `AggregateFunctionNullBase` parallel merge**

The thread-pool-aware `merge` in `AggregateFunctionNullBase` does not propagate the nullable flag from the source state to the destination, unlike the non-thread-pool `merge` which does. This was introduced when the thread-pool merge was first added and would cause incorrect NULL results if a future aggregate function both supports parallelized merge (`isAbleToParallelizeMerge`) and has `returns_default_when_only_null = false`. Currently all parallelizable functions (`uniqExact`, `uniqHLL12`) have `returns_default_when_only_null = true`, making `result_is_nullable` always false — so this path is compiled out.

Related: #100687

**2. Missing `ColumnFixedString` handling in JIT `getColumnData`**

When #100577 added JIT sort support for `ColumnString` and `ColumnFixedString`, it added a special case in `getColumnData` for `ColumnString` to always use the base chars pointer regardless of `skip_rows`. The matching `ColumnFixedString` case was missed. Currently no call site combines FixedString with `skip_rows > 0`, so the fallback `getDataAt(skip_rows)` always receives 0 and returns the correct pointer.

Related: #100577

### Changelog category (leave one):
- Not for changelog (changelog entry is not required)


### Changelog entry (a [user-readable short description](https://github.com/ClickHouse/ClickHouse/blob/master/docs/changelog_entry_guidelines.md) of the changes that goes into CHANGELOG.md):
...

### Documentation entry for user-facing changes

- [ ] Documentation is written (mandatory for new features)
author: Algunenano

commits.message:
Fix missing nullable flag propagation in parallel merge of `AggregateFunctionNullBase`

The non-parallel `merge` overload propagates the nullable result flag
(`setFlag`/`getFlag`) when `result_is_nullable` is true, but the parallel
`merge(... ThreadPool &...)` overload did not — it only delegated to the
nested function.

This is not currently exploitable because the only aggregate functions
that enable `isAbleToParallelizeMerge` are the `uniq*` family, and all of
them are registered with `returns_default_when_only_null = true`. This
causes `AggregateFunctionCombinatorNull::transformAggregateFunction` to
set `return_type_is_nullable = false`, so the Null combinator is always
instantiated as `AggregateFunctionNullUnary<result_is_nullable=false, ...>`
for any function reachable through the parallel merge path. The flag
propagation code lives inside `if constexpr (result_is_nullable)`, which
is compiled out entirely.

However, if a future aggregate function enables parallel merge without
setting `returns_default_when_only_null`, the missing flag propagation
would silently produce wrong nullable results (always NULL instead of the
correct aggregate value).

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

Handle `ColumnFixedString` in JIT `getColumnData` consistently with `ColumnString`

The `ColumnString` path in `getColumnData` was added in f3be927 to always
use the base chars pointer regardless of `skip_rows`, because the JIT
comparator uses absolute row indices. The matching `ColumnFixedString`
case was missed — the fallback `getDataAt(skip_rows)` would return an
offset pointer, which is incorrect when `skip_rows > 0` since the JIT
computes byte offsets as `row_index * n` from the base.

Currently no call site combines FixedString with `skip_rows > 0`, so
this is a defensive fix for consistency and safety.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

title: Fix incorrect argument types in AggregateFunction for optimized trivial count
body:
The optimized trivial count path in `applyTrivialCountIfPossible` was incorrectly using the storage column types as the aggregate function's argument types. For example, `count(v0 + v1)` where `v0` is `UInt32` and `v1` is `UInt64` would produce `AggregateFunction(count, UInt32, UInt64)` (the types of columns `v0` and `v1`) instead of the correct `AggregateFunction(count, UInt64)` (the type of the expression `v0 + v1`).

This causes the following exception when running such queries on a distributed table:

```
Code: 42. DB::Exception: Received from localhost:9000. DB::Exception: Aggregate function count requires zero or one argument: while receiving packet from 127.0.0.1:9000, local address: 127.0.0.1:39416: While executing Remote. (NUMBER_OF_ARGUMENTS_DOESNT_MATCH)
```

The fix uses `AggregateFunctionCount::getArgumentTypes` which already stores the correct argument types resolved during query analysis, instead of re-deriving them from the underlying storage columns.

### Changelog category (leave one):
- Bug Fix (user-visible misbehavior in an official stable release)

### Changelog entry (a [user-readable short description](https://github.com/ClickHouse/ClickHouse/blob/master/docs/changelog_entry_guidelines.md) of the changes that goes into CHANGELOG.md):
Fix incorrect `AggregateFunction` argument types in optimized trivial count, which caused `NUMBER_OF_ARGUMENTS_DOESNT_MATCH` exception when querying expressions like `count(v0 + v1)` on distributed tables.

### Documentation entry for user-facing changes

- [ ] Documentation is written (mandatory for new features)
author: YjyJeff

commits.message:
Fix incorrect argument types in DataTypeAggregateFunction for optimized trivial count

The optimized trivial count path in `applyTrivialCountIfPossible` was
incorrectly using the storage column types as the aggregate function's
argument types. For example, `count(v0 + v1)` would produce
`AggregateFunction(count, UInt32, UInt64)` (the types of columns `v0`
and `v1`) instead of the correct `AggregateFunction(count, UInt64)`
(the type of the expression `v0 + v1`).

Use `AggregateFunctionCount::getArgumentTypes` which already stores
the correct argument types resolved during analysis, instead of
re-deriving them from the underlying storage columns.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

Add test for trivial count aggregate function argument types

Verify that the optimized trivial count path produces correct argument
types in `AggregateFunction`. For `count(v0 + v1)` where `v0` is
`UInt32` and `v1` is `UInt64`, the type should be
`AggregateFunction(count, UInt64)`, not
`AggregateFunction(count, UInt32, UInt64)`.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

Add analyzer setting to trivial count argument types test

Explicitly enable the analyzer in the test to ensure consistent
behavior across configurations.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

Add distributed test for trivial count aggregate function argument types

Test that the optimized trivial count works correctly when querying
through a distributed table. Without the fix, the incorrect argument
types `AggregateFunction(count, UInt32, UInt64)` cause the following
exception when merging aggregation states across shards:

`Code: 42. DB::Exception: Received from localhost:9000. DB::Exception: Aggregate function count requires zero or one argument: while receiving packet from 127.0.0.1:9000, local address: 127.0.0.1:39416: While executing Remote. (NUMBER_OF_ARGUMENTS_DOESNT_MATCH)`

title: Fix segfault in dictionary internal query
body:
<!-- Fixes issue link part, do not remove -->

<!-- End of fixes issue link part, do not remove -->

### Changelog entry (a [user-readable short description](https://github.com/ClickHouse/ClickHouse/blob/master/docs/changelog_entry_guidelines.md) of the changes that goes into CHANGELOG.md):
Fix null pointer dereference segfault when loading dictionaries during server shutdown. `Context::getUserDefinedSQLObjectsStorage` (dereferences `user_defined_sql_objects_storage`) is called by dictionary threads concurrently with the main thread calling `Context::shutdown` (sets `user_defined_sql_objects_storage` to null). We need to make sure we disable future updates in the dictionaries loader, kill the currently running dictionary queries and join the dictionary loading threads - all before running `Context::shutdown`. Similar to what we do with normal queries.

title: auto-bisect: use portable base64 encoding for private CI credentials
body:
`base64 -w0` is GNU coreutils-specific. BSD/macOS `base64` does not accept `-w`, so `--private` mode fails on macOS with a cryptic error.

Switch to `printf '%s' ... | base64 | tr -d '\n'` which is POSIX-portable across Linux and macOS.

Followup to #100989.

### Changelog category (leave one):
- CI Fix or Improvement (changelog entry is not required)

### Changelog entry (a [user-readable short description](https://github.com/ClickHouse/ClickHouse/blob/master/docs/changelog_entry_guidelines.md) of the changes that goes into CHANGELOG.md):
...

### Documentation entry for user-facing changes

- [ ] Documentation is written (mandatory for new features)
author: fm4v

commits.message:
auto-bisect: use portable base64 encoding for private CI auth

`base64 -w0` is GNU-specific and fails on BSD/macOS where `base64`
has no `-w` flag. Switch to `printf '%s' ... | base64 | tr -d '\n'`
which is POSIX-portable and strips newlines on all platforms.

title: Optimize TRUNCATE DATABASE TABLES LIKE by pre-cancelling merges in parallel
body:
`TRUNCATE TABLES FROM db LIKE pattern` truncates matching tables via a
    thread pool. Previously, each thread sent a cancel signal and then waited
    up to 120 s for in-progress merges to finish, so tables queued behind a
    full thread pool had their merges running uncancelled until a thread became
    free. Total wait time was O(⌈N/K⌉ × max_merge_time).

    Fix: before entering the thread pool, iterate all matching tables and call
    `table->getActionLock(ActionLocks::PartsMerge)` on each. This atomically
    increments every table's `merges_blocker` counter at time 0, so all running
    merges and mutations receive the cancel signal simultaneously. By the time a
    thread-pool thread reaches `stopMergesAndWait()` for any table, the merge is
    already stopping or finished. Total wait time becomes O(max_merge_time)
    regardless of thread pool size or number of tables.

    The `ActionLock` objects are kept alive in `pre_merge_blockers` for the
    entire duration of the thread pool execution and released only after
    `waitForAllToFinishAndRethrowFirstError()` returns.

    Add regression test `03843_truncate_database_parallel_cancel` that creates
    five `MergeTree` tables, inserts enough parts to trigger background merges,
    runs `TRUNCATE TABLES FROM db LIKE 't%'`, and verifies that matched tables
    are empty and the unmatched table retains its data.

### Changelog category (leave one):
- Improvement

commits.message:
Fix out-of-bounds access in `getRequiredHeaderPositions` with projection PREWHERE

When a projection with PREWHERE is used alongside `optimizeLazyMaterialization2`,
the expression DAG can have more outputs than the `required_output_positions`
vector (sized from the sorting step's output header). The `_projection_filter`
column added by the projection inflates the DAG output count, causing an
out-of-bounds `vector<bool>` access and an abort in debug/sanitizer builds.

Cap the loop at `min(dag.getOutputs().size(), required_output_positions.size())`
so extra DAG outputs beyond the sorting header are safely ignored.
