title: Optimize itoa for Int128/UInt128: Barrett reduction and loop unrolling
body:
Replace the `__udivti3` compiler runtime call in the Int128/UInt128 `itoa` path with Barrett reduction, and unroll the conversion loop for UInt128.

**What changed:**

1. **Barrett reduction for 128-bit division by 10^18**: The `writeUIntText(UInt128)` path divided by 10^18 using `__udivti3`, a full software division routine. The compiler does not auto-optimize `unsigned __int128` division by a constant (unlike 64-bit, where it emits `imul`+`shr`), so we do it manually: multiply by the precomputed reciprocal `floor(2^128 / 10^18)` using schoolbook 64-bit multiplication, then correct with one remainder check. This eliminates all `__udivti3` calls from UInt128, Int128, UInt256, and Int256 paths.

2. **Unrolled UInt128 loop**: The `while (x > largest_uint64)` loop ran at most twice but the compiler generated a general loop that zero-initialized arrays, loaded constants, and allocated stack space *before* checking if the fast u64 path could be taken. Unrolled into explicit first/second division with helper functions. The compiler now defers all large-number setup past the early-exit branch.

3. **UInt256 left as a loop**: Unrolling UInt256 was tested and rejected — the 256-bit `_BitInt(256)` division code is too large to duplicate, causing 40-55% regression from icache pressure.

**Benchmarks:**

UInt128 (`toString(toUInt128(...))`):

| Case | Master | PR | Speedup |
|------|--------|----|---------|
| Small (fits u64, 1B rows) | 6.26s | 6.09s | 3% |
| Medium (1 div, 100M rows) | 1.77s | 1.55s | 12% |
| Large (2 divs, 100M rows) | 3.32s | 2.49s | 25% |

UInt256 (`toString(toUInt256(...))`) — no regression:

| Case | Master | PR | Speedup |
|------|--------|----|---------|
| Small (fits u64, 1B rows) | 6.26s | 6.09s | 3% |
| u128 range (1 div, 100M) | 1.77s | 1.55s | 12% |
| u128 range (2 divs, 100M) | 3.32s | 2.49s | 25% |
| 256-bit (1 div, 100M) | 27.09s | 26.20s | 3% |
| 256-bit (2 divs, 10M) | 7.01s | 6.99s | — |
| 256-bit (3 divs, 10M) | 14.48s | 14.23s | 2% |

### Changelog category (leave one):
- Performance Improvement

### Changelog entry (a [user-readable short description](https://github.com/ClickHouse/ClickHouse/blob/master/docs/changelog_entry_guidelines.md) of the changes that goes into CHANGELOG.md):
Faster Int128/UInt128 to string conversion by replacing software division with Barrett reduction and unrolling the conversion loop.

### Documentation entry for user-facing changes

- [ ] Documentation is written (mandatory for new features)
author: Algunenano

commits.message:
Replace `__udivti3` with Barrett reduction in Int128/Int256 itoa

The 128-bit itoa path divided by 10^18 using the compiler runtime
`__udivti3`, which is a full software division routine. Replace it
with Barrett reduction: multiply by the precomputed reciprocal
floor(2^128 / 10^18) using schoolbook 64-bit multiplication, then
correct the approximation with one remainder check.

This eliminates all `__udivti3` calls from UInt128, Int128, UInt256,
and Int256 itoa paths. ~7% faster for large UInt128 values, no
regression on small numbers.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

Unroll UInt128 itoa loop for better branch prediction and less wasted work

The `writeUIntText(UInt128)` loop ran at most twice but the compiler
generated a general loop that zero-initialized `two_values`, loaded
loop constants, and allocated stack space before checking if the fast
u64 path could be taken. This penalized the common small-number case.

Unroll the loop into explicit first/second division with helper functions
`extractDigitPairs` and `writeDigitPairs`. The compiler now defers all
large-number setup past the early-exit branch.

Benchmarks (1B small / 100M medium / 100M large UInt128 values):
  Small (fits u64):   4.25s → 3.78s  (12% faster)
  Medium (1 div):     1.54s → 1.13s  (27% faster)
  Large (2 divs):     2.71s → 2.41s  (11% faster)

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

Use extractDigitPairs/writeDigitPairs helpers in UInt256 itoa

Replace the inner digit extraction while-loop and output for-loop in
writeUIntText(UInt256) with the same helper functions used by UInt128.
Keeps the outer loop (unrolling was tested and rejected due to icache
pressure from the large 256-bit division code).

No performance regression; slightly cleaner code.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

Add `likely`/`unlikely` hints to itoa fast paths for small values

The compiler was placing callee-saved register pushes before the
early-exit branch in `writeUIntText(UInt128)`, causing a 14.5%
regression on small values that fit in 64 bits. Adding `likely` to
the `items[1] == 0` check guides the compiler to keep the fast path
(direct `jeaiii::to_text_from_integer`) before the register saves.

Also marks the second `divmod_1e18` call as `unlikely` since most
UInt128 values need only one division, and adds the same `likely`
hint to the UInt256 small-value fast path.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

title: Make min/max/argMin/argMax treat NaN consistently with ORDER BY
body:
Make `min`/`max`/`argMin`/`argMax` skip NaN values, matching `ORDER BY x ASC/DESC LIMIT 1` behavior. Previously, these aggregate functions used IEEE 754 comparisons where NaN is unordered, leading to inconsistent results depending on NaN position in the data. NaN is now always treated as last (skipped), and only returned when all values are NaN.

Also enables the `findExtremeMinIndex`/`MaxIndex` SIMD optimization for floating point `ORDER BY ... LIMIT 1` queries, which was previously disabled due to missing NaN handling.

Closes #72228

### Changelog category (leave one):
- Bug Fix (user-visible misbehavior in an official stable release)

### Changelog entry (a [user-readable short description](https://github.com/ClickHouse/ClickHouse/blob/master/docs/changelog_entry_guidelines.md) of the changes that goes into CHANGELOG.md):
`min`/`max`/`argMin`/`argMax` now treat NaN consistently with `ORDER BY`: NaN is always skipped (returned only when all values are NaN). Previously, results depended on NaN position in the data due to IEEE 754 unordered comparison semantics.

### Documentation entry for user-facing changes

- [ ] Documentation is written (mandatory for new features)
author: Algunenano

reviews.body: I'll review the rest later
Here's part 2/2
Nice! I've left one more comment
Very good to see this long-standing issue closed

commits.message:
Make `min`/`max`/`argMin`/`argMax` treat NaN consistently with ORDER BY

ClickHouse sorts NaN last regardless of sort direction. However,
`min`/`max`/`argMin`/`argMax` used IEEE 754 comparisons where NaN
is unordered, leading to inconsistent results depending on NaN
position in the data.

Fix all comparison paths to skip NaN (returning NaN only when all
values are NaN), matching `ORDER BY x ASC/DESC LIMIT 1` behavior:

- Scalar `setIfSmaller`/`setIfGreater`: use IEEE 754 trick
  `!(value <= candidate)` to handle NaN in a single comparison
- SIMD batch `findExtremeMin`/`Max`: post-process NaN results
  (only triggers when first element was NaN and "stuck")
- Index finding for `argMin`/`argMax`: NaN-aware scalar fallbacks
- JIT `compileMinMax`: `FCmpUNO` to detect NaN before comparison
- State merge: NaN-aware `setIfSmaller`/`setIfGreater` overloads

Also enables the `findExtremeMinIndex`/`MaxIndex` optimization for
floating point `ORDER BY LIMIT 1` (previously disabled), giving a
~2x speedup on that path.

Closes #72228

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

Update `02406_minmax_behaviour` reference for NaN-consistent min/max

The expected results for min/max with NaN arrays now reflect the new
behavior where NaN is always skipped (matching ORDER BY).

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

Fix pre-existing bug in `setSmallestNotNullIf`/`setGreatestNotNullIf` with both maps

When both `null_map` and `if_map` are present, the code computed
`final_flags = mergeIfAndNullFlags(...)` but then passed the raw
`if_map` to `findExtremeMinIf`/`findExtremeMaxIf` instead of
`final_flags.get()`, dropping the null filtering from the SIMD path.

Also adds a test for the combined Nullable + If case.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

Fix flaky `04055` test and update `01271` reference for NaN-consistent min/max

- `04055`: replace `UNION ALL` with `numbers(2)` in the "all NaN"
  argMin/argMax case — `UNION ALL` with constant subqueries has
  non-deterministic row ordering under randomized settings, causing
  the flaky checker to see `2 2` instead of `1 1` in ~50% of runs.

- `01271`: `max(n / abs(n))` where `n` includes 0 produces NaN from
  `0/0`, which previously won `max`. Now correctly returns `1`.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

Add comprehensive SIMD NaN tests for all conditional and Nullable variants

Add Section 7b with systematic coverage of all SIMD `findExtreme*`
paths: every function variant × NaN at first/middle/last position,
using 1000-row MergeTree tables to guarantee the SIMD batch path.

Covers: `min`/`max`, `argMin`/`argMax`, `minIf`/`maxIf`,
`argMinIf`/`argMaxIf`, Nullable `min`/`max`, Nullable `argMin`/`argMax`,
Nullable+If `minIf`/`maxIf`, Nullable+If `argMinIf`/`argMaxIf`
(27 test cases total). Verified stable over 100 flaky-check runs.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

Address review comments: simplify NaN handling, skip NaN at init

findExtreme.cpp:
- Skip NaN in the initial accumulator search inside `findExtremeImpl`
  instead of post-processing with `fixupNaNResult` (rienath's suggestion).
  Only one extra `isNaN` check on the first element for the no-NaN case.
  Removes `fixupNaNResult` entirely; public functions are simple one-liners.

SingleValueData.cpp:
- Simplify all 6 `setIfSmaller`/`setIfGreater` overloads to use a single
  IEEE 754 expression: `!isNaN(to) && !(value <= to)` which correctly
  rejects NaN input, replaces NaN accumulator, and does normal comparison.
- Remove redundant all-NaN handling from `setSmallest`/`setGreatest` and
  NotNullIf variants (already handled by `setIfSmaller`/`setIfGreater`).
- Remove unnecessary `else` after `if constexpr` with early return.
- Use `chassert(index <= row_end)` instead of silently handling impossible
  `index > row_end`.
- Simplify NaN-skip in `getSmallestIndexNotNullIf`/`getGreatestIndexNotNullIf`:
  save first valid index, single loop, no re-scan.
- Use `candidate` directly instead of re-reading via `set(column, ...)`.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

title: Keeper: fix race between read requests and session close
body:
### Changelog category (leave one):
- Bug Fix (user-visible misbehavior in an official stable release)

### Changelog entry (a [user-readable short description](https://github.com/ClickHouse/ClickHouse/blob/master/docs/changelog_entry_guidelines.md) of the changes that goes into CHANGELOG.md):
Fixed a bug in Keeper where a read request could get stuck (causing session to time out) if a different unrelated session on the same server was closed at just the wrong moment.

---


`KeeperDispatcher::finishSession` used to remove the session id from `read_request_queue`. But the requests in `read_request_queue` don't necessarily belong this session, they're just piggy-backed to this session's requests. So read requests could be dropped incorrectly and never completed.

Simple fix: just don't do anything to `read_request_queue` in `KeeperDispatcher::finishSession`. Requests would be removed from it naturally when they complete.

The added test fails without the fix.
author: al13n321

commits.message: Keeper: fix race between read requests and session close
Cancel dependent reads on write error

title: Allow `use_partition_pruning` setting to also disable `MinMax` index on partition key columns
body:
This will be useful to also detect bugs due to partition's minmax index condition such as:

```sql
CREATE TABLE t (x Int32) ENGINE = MergeTree PARTITION BY x ORDER BY tuple();
INSERT INTO t VALUES (0), (1), (2);

SELECT * FROM t WHERE x != 0 AND intDiv(100, x) > 10; -- throws Division by zero. (ILLEGAL_DIVISION)
```
We can now see that it was because of partition's minmax index condition:

```sql
CREATE TABLE t (x Int32) ENGINE = MergeTree PARTITION BY x ORDER BY tuple();
INSERT INTO t VALUES (0), (1), (2);

SELECT * FROM t WHERE x != 0 AND intDiv(100, x) > 10 SETTINGS use_partition_pruning = 0; -- works
```

### Changelog category (leave one):
- Improvement


### Changelog entry (a [user-readable short description](https://github.com/ClickHouse/ClickHouse/blob/master/docs/changelog_entry_guidelines.md) of the changes that goes into CHANGELOG.md):
`use_partition_pruning = 0` now also disables `MinMax` index pruning and count optimization on partition key columns, in addition to disabling pruning based on partition keys.

### Documentation entry for user-facing changes

title: Fix `divide` and `intDiv` monotonicity causing exception during index analysis
body:
`divide` and `intDiv` incorrectly returned that it is monotonic when denominator range is `[0, 0]`, leading to division by zero exception in queries such as:

```sql
CREATE TABLE test_int_divide (x Int32) ENGINE = MergeTree PARTITION BY x ORDER BY tuple();
INSERT INTO test_int_divide VALUES (0), (1), (2), (3), (4);

SELECT * FROM test_int_divide WHERE x != 0 AND intDiv(10, x) > 4 ORDER BY x; -- throws ILLEGAL_DIVISION
```

```sql
CREATE TABLE test_divide (x Decimal32(2)) ENGINE = MergeTree PARTITION BY x ORDER BY tuple();
INSERT INTO test_divide VALUES (0), (1), (2);

SELECT * FROM test_divide WHERE x != 0 AND divide(10, x) > 4 ORDER BY x; -- throws ILLEGAL_DIVISION
```

### Changelog category (leave one):
- Bug Fix (user-visible misbehavior in an official stable release)


### Changelog entry (a [user-readable short description](https://github.com/ClickHouse/ClickHouse/blob/master/docs/changelog_entry_guidelines.md) of the changes that goes into CHANGELOG.md):

title: Fix several minor bugs in text index
body:
### Changelog category (leave one):
- Bug Fix (user-visible misbehavior in an official stable release)

### Changelog entry (a [user-readable short description](https://github.com/ClickHouse/ClickHouse/blob/master/docs/changelog_entry_guidelines.md) of the changes that goes into CHANGELOG.md):
Fixed analysis of predicates with the `IN` function by text index with the preprocessor. Fixed the collision of searched tokens in the text index, which could lead to incorrect results.
author: CurtizJ

commits.message:
Fix three bugs in text index implementation

1. Fix buffer position not advanced after zero-copy Roaring bitmap read
   in `PostingsSerialization::deserialize`. The fast path that reads
   directly from the `ReadBuffer` memory did not advance `istr.position()`
   past the consumed bytes, which would corrupt subsequent sequential reads.

2. Fix preprocessor not applied to IN-set values in
   `MergeTreeIndexConditionText::tryPrepareSetForTextSearch`. When a text
   index has a preprocessor (e.g. `lower()`), the index stores preprocessed
   tokens. The IN path tokenized raw set values without preprocessing,
   causing false-negative granule skips and missing rows in query results.

3. Fix `TextSearchQuery::getHash` collision for queries with different token
   boundaries but identical concatenated bytes (e.g. `['abc','def']` vs
   `['abcd','ef']`). Hash each token's length before its content to ensure
   distinct hashes.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

Update test references for changed `TextSearchQuery` hash

The hash fix in `TextSearchQuery::getHash` (now includes token lengths)
changes the hex suffix in `__text_index_*` virtual column names. Update
all affected reference files to match the new hashes.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

Fix typo and numerical overflow in text index code

1. Fix typo `is_fully_materialied` → `is_fully_materialized` in
   `optimizeDirectReadFromTextIndex.cpp` (3 occurrences).

2. Fix potential `double` overflow in `estimateCardinality` for
   `TextSearchMode::All`. The previous code multiplied token
   cardinalities directly (e.g. 5M^50 for 50 tokens), which overflows
   `double`. Compute in log-space instead:
   `exp(sum(log(|Ai|)) - (n-1) * log(N))`.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

title: ci: allow using customized sccache storage.
body:
Adds support in praktika to (re)define local env variables for local job runs

Allow developers to use their own sccache storage. 

### Changelog category (leave one):
- CI Fix or Improvement (changelog entry is not required)
author: RinChanNOWWW

reviews.body: Generally, I like the idea of using ccache in local runs; thank you for your contribution

commits.message:
ci: support local env overrides via ci/local.env

Add a generic `ci/local.env` mechanism to `praktika` that lets developers
set environment variables for local CI runs without touching project-specific
CI code or checked-in settings.

- `runner.py` reads `ci/local.env` (gitignored, `KEY=VALUE` format) at the
  start of `run()` and exports the variables into the current process
- For Docker jobs, `--env-file ci/local.env` is passed to `docker run` so
  the same variables reach the container
- `ci/local.env.example` documents the file format and shows the sccache
  use case
- `settings.py`: removed project-specific `SCCACHE_S3_*` fields
- `build_clickhouse.py`: removed the `Settings.SCCACHE_*` indirection;
  credentials are now passed directly via `ci/local.env`

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

ci: fix fast_test.py to use env-based sccache config

Remove the remaining `Settings.SCCACHE_*` references introduced in the
original PR, consistent with the generic `ci/local.env` approach.

title: Fix wrong results for integer boolean predicates in WHERE on MergeTree
body:
### Changelog category (leave one):
- Bug Fix (user-visible misbehavior in an official stable release)

### Changelog entry (a user-readable short description of the changes that goes to CHANGELOG.md):
Fix wrong query results when a large integer constant (e.g. 256, 2147483648) is used as a boolean predicate in a WHERE clause with AND on MergeTree tables. For example, `SELECT count() FROM t WHERE (2147483648 > b) AND 2147483648` would incorrectly return 0 instead of matching all rows.

### Summary

When a non-UInt8 integer constant is used as a boolean predicate in a WHERE clause with AND (e.g. `WHERE (256 > b) AND 256`), MergeTree incorrectly prunes all parts and returns 0 rows. This affects any integer value where `value % 256 == 0` (256, 512, 65536, 2147483648, etc.).

**Root cause:** `splitFilterNodeForAllowedInputs()` in `VirtualColumnUtils.cpp` reduces `AND(expr_with_column, constant)` to just `constant` when the column is not a virtual column. Since the constant's type (e.g. UInt16) differs from the AND function's return type (UInt8), a numeric cast `CAST(256, 'UInt8')` is applied, truncating 256 → 0 (256 mod 256). This makes `filterPartsByVirtualColumns()` see `always_false` and prune all parts.

**Fix:** Replace the truncating numeric cast with `notEquals(x, 0)`, which correctly converts any numeric value to boolean (UInt8 0/1) without truncation. Values where `value % 256 == 0` now correctly evaluate to true (1).

Closes https://github.com/ClickHouse/ClickHouse/issues/101269
Closes https://github.com/ClickHouse/ClickHouse/issues/99979

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
author: groeneai

commits.message:
Fix wrong results for integer boolean predicates in WHERE on MergeTree

When a large integer constant is used as a boolean predicate in a WHERE
clause with AND on a MergeTree table, e.g. `WHERE (256 > b) AND 256`,
the query incorrectly returns 0 rows instead of matching all rows.

Root cause: `splitFilterNodeForAllowedInputs()` in VirtualColumnUtils.cpp
reduces `AND(greater(256, b), 256)` to just `256` when column `b` is not
an allowed input for virtual column filtering. Since the remaining child's
type (UInt16) differs from the AND's return type (UInt8), it applies a
numeric cast: `CAST(256, 'UInt8')` = 0 (truncation). This makes
`filterPartsByVirtualColumns()` see always_false, pruning all parts.

Fix: Replace the truncating numeric cast with `notEquals(x, 0)`, which
correctly converts any numeric value to boolean (0 or 1) without
truncation. Values like 256, 512, 65536, 2147483648 now correctly
evaluate to true.

Closes #101269

Fix arm_tidy build: remove unnecessary std::move on ColumnPtr

The performance-move-const-arg clang-tidy check flags std::move()
on ColumnPtr (immutable_ptr<IColumn> wrapping boost::intrusive_ptr<const IColumn>).
Copying the intrusive pointer is just an atomic refcount increment,
so the move is unnecessary. Removing it fixes the arm_tidy CI failure.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

title: Fix flaky test 03800_assume_not_null_coalesce_if_null_monotonicity_key_condition
body:
### Changelog category (leave one):
- CI Fix or Improvement (changelog entry is not required)

### Changelog entry (a]notation for the changelog):
- none

---

## Summary

Pin `query_plan_merge_expressions = 1` in the test to prevent EXPLAIN output mismatches when CI randomizes this setting to 0.

- The test verifies that `assumeNotNull`/`coalesce`/`ifNull` monotonicity is correctly used by KeyCondition for MinMax index and primary key condition optimization
- `query_plan_merge_expressions` only controls cosmetic merging of adjacent expression steps in the query plan — it does not affect index analysis or granule pruning
- When randomized to 0, all 10+ EXPLAIN blocks produce structurally different output (unmerged expression nodes), breaking the reference comparison

## CIDB evidence

13 failures across 6 unrelated PRs in 30 days (zero master failures — only triggered by CI randomization in PR runs):
- PR #100874 (settings randomization PR), PR #99513, PR #96130, PR #99181, PR #94017, PR #98500

## Reproduction

Deterministic reproduction:
```
CLICKHOUSE_CLIENT_OPT="--query_plan_merge_expressions 0" \
  tests/clickhouse-test --no-random-settings --no-random-merge-tree-settings \
  03800_assume_not_null_coalesce_if_null_monotonicity_key_condition
# → FAIL every time without fix
# → OK every time with fix
```

Verified 50/50 passes with full randomization after fix.
author: groeneai

commits.message:
Fix flaky test 03800_assume_not_null_coalesce_if_null_monotonicity_key_condition

Pin query_plan_merge_expressions=1 to prevent EXPLAIN output mismatch when CI
randomizes this setting to 0. The test verifies key condition monotonicity for
assumeNotNull/coalesce/ifNull — expression merging is cosmetic and irrelevant
to the tested optimization.

Reproduces deterministically with --query_plan_merge_expressions 0.
Verified 50/50 passes with full randomization after fix.

<!-- CURSOR_SUMMARY -->
---

> [!NOTE]
> **Medium Risk**
> Changes user authentication parsing and validation, which can affect login behavior and security-related configuration errors. Risk is mitigated by extensive new unit/integration coverage but misconfigurations may now be rejected differently.
> 
> **Overview**
> Adds support for specifying **multiple authentication methods per user** in `users.xml` via a new `<auth_methods>` section, appending each parsed method into `User::authentication_methods`.
> 
> Refactors auth parsing into `parseUserAuthMethod()` and tightens validation: forbids mixing `<auth_methods>` with flat auth fields, disallows `no_password` inside `<auth_methods>` (and combining it with other methods), enforces OTP usage only when at least one password-based method exists, and improves `http_authentication` required-field error handling.
> 
> Adds comprehensive gtests for multi-auth parsing and new integration tests covering multi-password/mixed auth logins plus an SSL+password mixed user scenario.
> 
> <sup>Written by [Cursor Bugbot](https://cursor.com/dashboard?tab=bugbot) for commit 7423423d87dc9363261e2245647bd1ae5502f8d7. This will update automatically on new commits. Configure [here](https://cursor.com/dashboard?tab=bugbot).</sup>
<!-- /CURSOR_SUMMARY -->

title: Undo Groene AI collageral damage
body:
Our new stochastic friend Groene AI caused collateral damage. It fixed wrong results with `query_plan_merge_expressions = 0` by pinning the setting value to 1. In one case, @Ergus fixed wrong results by changing C++ code. Relevant PRs:

- https://github.com/ClickHouse/ClickHouse/pull/100909
- https://github.com/ClickHouse/ClickHouse/pull/100937
- https://github.com/ClickHouse/ClickHouse/pull/101316
- https://github.com/ClickHouse/ClickHouse/pull/101592
- https://github.com/ClickHouse/ClickHouse/pull/101573

However,
- `query_plan_merge_expressions` was at no point randomized,
- ... and it also should not, its docs say `This is an expert-level setting which should only be used for debugging by developers` and we don't expect users to disable it in real-world use cases.
- Finally, there is an [open PR](https://github.com/ClickHouse/ClickHouse/pull/100874/) which previously _did_ randomize the setting (this is reverted by now). Groene AI based its many fix PRs on this other open PR. This should now also be fixed by now (only merged PRs are considered).

As per this PR, the collateral damage is removed in the interest of less clutter in the code and tests.

title: Add `arrayAutocorrelation` function
body:
Relates to #87209

### Changelog category (leave one):
- New Feature


### Changelog entry (a [user-readable short description](https://github.com/ClickHouse/ClickHouse/blob/master/docs/changelog_entry_guidelines.md) of the changes that goes into CHANGELOG.md):
Add function `arrayAutocorrelation(arr [, max_lag])` that computes the normalized autocorrelation of a numeric array for each lag. Supports integer, float, and decimal array types. 

### Documentation entry for user-facing changes

- [x] Documentation is written (mandatory for new features)

<!---
Directly edit documentation source files in the "docs" folder with the same pull-request as code changes

or

Add a user-readable short description of the changes that should be added to docs.clickhouse.com below.

At a minimum, the following information should be added (but add more as needed).
- Motivation: Why is this function, table engine, etc. useful to ClickHouse users?

- Parameters: If the feature being added takes arguments, options or is influenced by settings, please list them below with a brief explanation.

- Example use: A query or command.
-->

- Motivation: ClickHouse's [aggregation functions](https://clickhouse.com/docs/sql-reference/aggregate-functions) already support linear/logistic regression, their stochastic versions, and some statistical tests. However, this is not enough for frequent scenarios of building predictive models, which is why we have to build ETL processes. To improve performance, it would be beneficial to move the implementation of at least one complete use case directly into the DBMS (like ELT). The Auto Correlation Function (ACF) is essential for time-series analysis within ClickHouse. It allows users to detect seasonality, periodic patterns, and trends in sequential data (like stock prices, server metrics, or sensor readings) without needing to export data to Python or R.

- Parameters: arr: An array of numeric values (Array(Int*) or Array(Float*)). The function automatically casts integers to Float64 for high-precision calculation.

- Example use: SELECT arrayAutocorrelation([10, 20, 30, 10, 20, 30]);
- Result: [1, -0.25, -0.5, 0.5, 0, -0.25]
- (Index 0 is Lag 0, Index 1 is Lag 1, etc.)
author: wenyuchen96

reviews.body:
Thanks for working on the feature! Most of it looks good!

I noticed that the time complexity of the function per-row is `O(n^2)` where `n` is the size of the array. We should mention that in the documentation. Additionally, the other issue is that if the array contains millions of elements, which is not very uncommon, the function will never finish executing.

There are some advanced `O(nlogn)` algorithms but they may be bit complicated which we can add later as an improvement.  However, since normally users are only interested in the result of first few lags, we can offer them the signature `arrayAutocorrelation(array, max_lag)`; then the time complexity will become `O(n * max_lag)`. We can keep the `max_lag` optional and if it's not given then we compute result for all lags. In that way, if `max_lag` is small, our function will be still be useful even if array contains millions of elements.

Looks good. Previous issues are mostly resolved. I found some queries that do not work as expected:

If we mix const column (first argument) and non-const column for second argument, it cannot handle it. You can have a look at `src/Functions/array/arrayDotProduct.cpp` for an example of how to handle when one of the argument is constant. 
```sql
SELECT arrayAutocorrelation([1,2,3], number) FROM numbers(3);
```
Gives:
```
Code: 44. DB::Exception: Argument must be an array: while executing function arrayAutocorrelation on arguments _CAST([1, 2, 3]_Array(UInt8), 'Array(UInt8)'_String) Array(UInt8) Const(size = 0, Array(size = 1, UInt64(size = 1), UInt8(size = 3))), __table1.number UInt64 UInt64(size = 0). (ILLEGAL_COLUMN)
```

Since we read `max_lag` via `getInt(i)`, if we pass a big `UInt64`, we will wrap around and produce incorrect result silently.
```sql
SELECT arrayAutocorrelation([1, 2, 3], toUInt64('9223372036854775808'))
```
Gives incorrectly `[]`. You can look at `src/Functions/repeat.cpp` for an example of how we can get the numbers column properly.

For the following query, I was expecting it to return `[]` because the rule seems to be output array length = `min(array_size, max_lag)` . But returns `[nan]`.
```sql
SELECT arrayAutocorrelation([1], 0);
```

commits.message:
feat(Functions): add arrayAutocorrelation function

Relates to #87209
fix: resolve style issues and accepted the review comments about introduced_in
fix: actually save changes for whitespace and version 26.2
refactor: use range-based for loop for offsets
fix line 104-113: use pointer arithmetic for UBSAN failure
Docs: Add minimal stub to satisfy CI
Fix: Add comment to satisfy doc check
Fix: Handle const columns, large integers, and edge cases
Do not allow negative max lag
Allow Int128/Int256 lag types as well
Add test for low cardinality and nullable types
Do not support Int128/UInt256 types max lag

title: Fix flaky test 02346_text_index_prewhere_support
body:
Pin `query_plan_remove_unused_columns = 1` in the EXPLAIN query to stabilize skip index
EXPLAIN output when the setting is randomized to False.

When `query_plan_remove_unused_columns` is False (newly randomized by #100874), unused
columns stay in the query plan. This shifts text index INPUT node positions from 0 to 1+
and drops the `Actions:` prefix, causing the reference file comparison to fail. The setting
is pinned only in the EXPLAIN subquery (query-level SETTINGS) — actual query execution and
data correctness checks are unaffected.

17 failures across 7 unrelated PRs in 30 days.

### Changelog category (leave one):
- CI Fix or Improvement (changelog entry is not required)

### Changelog entry (a user-readable short description of the changes that goes into CHANGELOG.md):
...

### Documentation entry for user-facing changes

- [ ] Documentation is written (mandatory for new features)
author: groeneai

commits.message:
Fix flaky test 02346_text_index_prewhere_support

Pin `query_plan_remove_unused_columns = 1` in the EXPLAIN query to
stabilize skip index EXPLAIN output.

When `query_plan_remove_unused_columns` is randomized to False (by PR
#100874's settings randomizer), unused columns remain in the query plan,
shifting text index INPUT node positions from 0 to 1+ and removing the
"Actions:" prefix from the output. This causes the reference file
comparison to fail.

The fix pins the setting only in the EXPLAIN query (query-level SETTINGS),
so it does not affect the actual query execution or data correctness checks.
The test still verifies that text indexes work correctly in PREWHERE with
both skip_indexes=0 and skip_indexes=1.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

Apply query_plan_remove_unused_columns at file scope

Move the setting from per-query SETTINGS clause to MY_CLICKHOUSE_CLIENT
definition, as requested by reviewer @rschu1ze.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

title: Fix undefined behaviour in DateTimeBestEffort parsing
body:
parseDateTimeBestEffort accumulated fractional-second digits into an Int64 without capping the count, causing signed integer overflow.
                                                                                                                                                
Fix: cap fractional digit count to std::numeric_limits<FractionalType>::digits10 at all three call sites. Also consistently use if constexpr (strict) since strict is a template parameter.

Related CI failure: https://github.com/ClickHouse/ClickHouse/actions/runs/23661784718/job/68936469304?pr=100435#step:4:11003
 
### Changelog category (leave one):                                                                                                                 
- Bug Fix (user-visible misbehavior in an official stable release)                                                                              

### Changelog entry (a user-readable short description of the changes that goes into CHANGELOG.md):                                                 

Fix undefined behavior (signed integer overflow) in parseDateTimeBestEffort when parsing datetime strings with more than 18 fractional-second  digits.
author: bacek

commits.message:
Fix undefined behaviour in DateTimeBestEffort parsing

1. Follow same pattern which already existed.
2. Consistently use "if constexpr (strict)"

Add regression test for UB fix in `parseDateTimeBestEffort`

Test that parsing datetime strings with many fractional digits (more than
`Int64::digits10 = 18`) does not cause signed integer overflow in
`readDecimalNumber`. Covers 10-digit and 9-digit unix timestamps with
fractional parts, and regular datetime strings.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

Clarify regression test: add explicit 19-digit case and fix boundary comment

`readDigits` reads up to `UInt64::digits10 = 19` digits into its buffer,
so a 19-digit fractional tail is consumed and truncated to 18 (silently
dropped), while a 20-digit tail leaves the 20th digit in the stream and
causes a parse error. Add the 19-digit case to lock in the expected
truncation behavior and update the comment to reflect the actual boundary.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

Fix regression test to actually reproduce the UB

The previous test used fractional values that fit in Int64 even without the
cap, so the bugfix validator (which runs the test against master) correctly
reported "failed to reproduce the bug".

Use 19 nines as the fractional part: without the cap, `readDecimalNumber`
processes chunks 4+4+4+4+3 and the final step `9999999999999999 * 1000`
overflows Int64. With the fix the count is capped to 18 (chunks 4+4+4+4+2)
and no overflow occurs.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

title: Do not invalidate dictionary if there is only one of them
body:
There are cases where having one big dictionary helps quite a bit:

* `low_cardinality_use_single_dictionary_for_part=1`
* `low_cardinality_max_dictionary_size=1048576`

Prior to this patch discontinuous reads caused repeated invalidation of the dictionary, which gets expensive when a dictionary is large. With this change the dictionary is only invalidated if there's more than one dictionary associated with the part.

Closes #99236.

### Changelog category (leave one):
- Performance Improvement

### Changelog entry (a [user-readable short description](https://github.com/ClickHouse/ClickHouse/blob/master/docs/changelog_entry_guidelines.md) of the changes that goes into CHANGELOG.md):

Faster discontinuous queries for `LowCardinality` columns with a single dictionary.

### Documentation entry for user-facing changes

- [ ] Documentation is written (mandatory for new features)

<!---
Directly edit documentation source files in the "docs" folder with the same pull-request as code changes

or

Add a user-readable short description of the changes that should be added to docs.clickhouse.com below.

At a minimum, the following information should be added (but add more as needed).
- Motivation: Why is this function, table engine, etc. useful to ClickHouse users?

- Parameters: If the feature being added takes arguments, options or is influenced by settings, please list them below with a brief explanation.

- Example use: A query or command.
-->

<!-- CURSOR_SUMMARY -->
---

> [!NOTE]
> **Medium Risk**
> Touches LowCardinality bulk deserialization state and dictionary refresh logic; an incorrect single-dictionary detection could lead to stale dictionaries or read errors in edge cases.
> 
> **Overview**
> Improves performance of discontinuous reads for `LowCardinality` columns by **not invalidating/re-reading the cached per-part dictionary** when the part is detected to contain exactly one dictionary (tracked via a new `single_dictionary_for_part` flag set after the first dictionary read reaches EOF).
> 
> Adds a stateless regression test that exercises a selective `PREWHERE` query and asserts bounded `ReadCompressedBytes` to catch dictionary re-decompression/read amplification, plus a correctness check for multi-dictionary cases.
> 
> <sup>Written by [Cursor Bugbot](https://cursor.com/dashboard?tab=bugbot) for commit 0c4cefe72e655253880633d8af2bdf8b2c0d316a. This will update automatically on new commits. Configure [here](https://cursor.com/dashboard?tab=bugbot).</sup>
<!-- /CURSOR_SUMMARY -->
author: bobrik

reviews.body: Cursor Bugbot has reviewed your changes and found 1 potential issue.

Sounds good!

Also, I'd like to have input from @KochetovNicolai - maybe there is a more generic way to solve it? By not re-reading dictionary if it appears to be the same.
Tests have failed, and CI found memory safety issues.
This is very interesting! But I'd also like to ask @KochetovNicolai for review.

The idea is really good — detecting single-dictionary parts via marks and skipping redundant re-reads is the right approach. However, I think the current implementation has two main disadvantages:

**1) In multi-threaded reads from the same part, the dictionary is still read multiple times**

Right now the dictionary is read independently by every thread during the first granule's processing (`!global_dictionary` is true in `deserializeBinaryBulkWithMultipleStreams`). If 8 threads read the same part, each one reads the dictionary from disk once and keeps its own copy in memory.

We can fix this by reading the dictionary in `deserializeBinaryBulkStatePrefix` instead. For Wide parts, `use_specialized_prefixes_and_suffixes_substreams` is false, so the prefix and data share the same `DictionaryKeys` stream. After reading the version (8 bytes), the stream is positioned exactly at the start of the dictionary data — we can read it right there.

`MergeTreeReaderWide` already has a `DeserializationPrefixesCache` that is shared across all readers of the same part. The first reader calls `deserializeBinaryBulkStatePrefix` (which would now read the dictionary), and all subsequent readers get cloned states via `DeserializationPrefixesCache::clonePrefixes`. The clone copies `global_dictionary` as a `shared_ptr<const IColumnUnique>`, so all threads share the same const dictionary object — no memory duplication and no per-thread disk reads.

The existing condition in `deserializeBinaryBulkWithMultipleStreams` already handles the rest: `single_dictionary_for_part=true` and `global_dictionary != null` means `read_dictionary()` is never called, and the `DictionaryKeys` stream is effectively unused during per-granule processing.

**2) The `low_cardinality_single_dictionary_for_column` map is too specific**

The current approach with a per-column map in `MergeTreeReaderWide` and special detection logic in `addStream` is tightly coupled to LowCardinality. It only works for top-level LowC
Looks great, thanks!

commits.message:
Do not invalidate dictionary if there is only one of them

There are cases where having one big dictionary helps quite a bit:

* `low_cardinality_use_single_dictionary_for_part=1`
* `low_cardinality_max_dictionary_size=1048576`

Prior to this patch discontinuous reads caused repeated invalidation of the dictionary,
which gets expensive when a dictionary is large. With this change the dictionary is only
invalidated if there's more than one dictionary associated with the part.

Signed-off-by: Ivan Babrou <github@ivan.computer>
Merge branch 'master' of github.com:ClickHouse/ClickHouse into ivan/single-dict-no-reload

title: improvement: use INCORRECT_DATA instead of LOGICAL_ERROR for data-reachable deserialization checks
body:
In sanitizer and debug builds, `LOGICAL_ERROR` calls `abortOnFailedAssertion` → `abort()` before the exception propagates to the caller's `catch(...)`. The row-count mismatch check in `NativeReader` is reachable from user-supplied binary data and should use `INCORRECT_DATA` so it is recognized as a data error rather than a logic error, preventing false-positive aborts during fuzzing.

### Changes

- **`NativeReader.cpp`**: `LOGICAL_ERROR` → `INCORRECT_DATA` for row count mismatch after column deserialization (user-supplied Native format stream).
- **`SerializationObject.h`**: add explicit `#include <Common/VectorWithMemoryTracking.h>` (previously relied on transitive include).
- **`ColumnUnique.h`**: typo fix ("grated" → "greater") in error message; error code unchanged.
- **`SerializationArray.cpp`**: error code declaration reorder only; no functional change.

### Changelog category (leave one):
- Bug Fix (user-visible misbehavior in an official stable release)

### Changelog entry (a user-readable short description of the changes that goes into CHANGELOG.md):

Fix false-positive abort in `NativeReader` when deserializing a Native format stream with a row-count mismatch: changed from `LOGICAL_ERROR` to `INCORRECT_DATA` so the error is handled as a data error rather than triggering `abort()` in sanitizer/debug builds.

### Documentation entry for user-facing changes

- [ ] Documentation is written (mandatory for new features)

CI report: https://s3.amazonaws.com/clickhouse-test-reports/PRs/99822/76660ad7a27047de3d00837309ab28f384318007/result_pr.json
author: motsc

reviews.body:
I don't understand these changes. Almost all `LOGICAL_ERRORS` that were changed to `INCORRECT_DATA` in this PR are actual logical errors that can happen only because of bugs in deserialization from MergeTree parts. `INCORRECT_DATA` makes sense only for cases when we use Native format reader and data is serialized incorrectly. As you can see, in some places we even check for `settings.native_format` to decide do we need to throw `INCORRECT_DATA` or `LOGICAL_ERRORS`. The only place where change can be justified is probably lines 198 and 224 in `SerializationReplicated.cpp`.

If we just change all these `LOGICAL_ERRORS` to `INCORRECT_DATA` we might silently ignore actual bugs in the code, and we have alerts on `LOGICAL_ERRORS` both in CI and in production.
Let's also improve the PR description as it contains old changes

commits.message:
fix: replace LOGICAL_ERROR with INCORRECT_DATA in deserialization paths

In ASan and fuzzer builds, `LOGICAL_ERROR` calls `abortOnFailedAssertion`
→ `abort()` before the exception propagates to the caller's `catch(...)`.
Any condition that can be triggered by malformed binary input must use
`INCORRECT_DATA` (or another non-`LOGICAL_ERROR` code) so it throws a
catchable exception instead of aborting the process.

The following sites were changed because the condition is fully determined
by the content of the data stream, not by a programming error:

- `NativeReader.cpp`: row count mismatch after column deserialization
- `SerializationNullable.cpp`: null map / nested column size mismatch
  (was `native_format ? INCORRECT_DATA : LOGICAL_ERROR`; the non-native
  branch is also reachable from malformed input)
- `SerializationTuple.cpp`: tuple element size mismatch after deserialization
  (same `native_format` conditional pattern)
- `SerializationArray.cpp`: element column longer than last offset value
- `SerializationSparse.cpp`: inconsistent offsets / values sizes
- `SerializationString.cpp`: empty data stream in bulk deserialization
- `SerializationObject.cpp`: missing data stream and size mismatches in
  `STRING`, typed, and dynamic deserialization paths
- `SerializationObjectSharedData.cpp`: ~20 sites across `deserializeStructure`,
  `deserializePathsInfos`, `deserializePathsData`, and bucket deserialization —
  all "got empty stream", row-count mismatches, and path-not-found conditions
- `SerializationReplicated.cpp`: `num_rows != limit` (read from stream),
  invalid `size_of_indexes_type`, missing `ReplicatedElements` substream
- `ColumnUnique.h`: null in non-nullable dictionary; index out of range in
  dictionary lookup
- `Allocator.cpp`: `checkSize` threw `LOGICAL_ERROR` for sizes ≥ 2^63;
  changed to `CANNOT_ALLOCATE_MEMORY` since the condition is triggered by
  an attacker-controlled allocation size, not a bug in the allocator itself

Serialization-side throws and invariant guards that represent true programming
errors (e.g., uninitialized callbacks, wrong codec mode) are left as
`LOGICAL_ERROR`.

Discovered by fuzzing with `native_reader_fuzzer` and
`nested_type_serialization_fuzzer` in ASan build.

**Bug Fix (user-visible misbehavior in official stable or prestable release)**
- Fixed process abort in Native format deserialization when reading malformed data containing inconsistent column sizes, missing substreams, or other structural errors.

fix: revert accidental constructor refactoring in SerializationObject.cpp

The previous commit accidentally included an incomplete refactoring of
SerializationObject that removed constructor parameters from the .cpp but
not from the .h or call sites, causing a compilation error. Restore
SerializationObject.cpp to match origin/master's constructor signature
while preserving the three LOGICAL_ERROR → INCORRECT_DATA changes.

fix: restore 4-arg constructor signature in SerializationObjectSharedData and fix typo

- Match .cpp constructor definition to .h declaration by restoring the
  `dynamic_serialization_` parameter (was accidentally dropped, causing
  an out-of-line definition mismatch that breaks compilation).

- Fix typo in `ColumnUnique::checkIndexes` exception message:
  `grated or equal` → `greater or equal`.

fix: remove unused LOGICAL_ERROR declarations and broken values_settings in SerializationSparse

- Remove unused `extern const int LOGICAL_ERROR` from four files where all
  throw sites were changed to `INCORRECT_DATA` in the prior commit but the
  declaration was left behind, causing style-check failures.

- Remove `values_settings.insert_only_rows_in_current_range_from_substreams_cache`
  block added by accident to `SerializationSparse::deserializeBinaryBulkWithMultipleStreams`.
  The flag, when propagated to nested serializers, caused the `Dynamic` element
  of a `Tuple` to read 0 rows from the substream cache instead of 1, producing an
  "Unexpected size of tuple element" mismatch during `CHECK TABLE` on a table with
  sparse-serialized `Tuple(Dynamic, Tuple(Int))` columns.
  This regressed test `04038_check_table_sparse_tuple_dynamic`.

fix: restore deserializeOffsets early return for limit=0 in SerializationSparse

Commit e3380323970 accidentally removed the 'if (max_rows_to_read == 0) return 0'
early return that was introduced by PR #99351 to fix CHECK TABLE on Tuple columns
containing Dynamic elements with sparse-serialized sub-columns (issue #96588).

The removed guard was replaced with 'if (max_rows_to_read && ...)' guards on the
two conditions below, but those do not short-circuit the function: the read loop
still executes when limit=0, reading an extra row instead of returning immediately.
This regresses test 04038_check_table_sparse_tuple_dynamic.

Restore the original early return and remove the spurious 'max_rows_to_read &&'
guard from the loop condition, since it is now unreachable when limit=0.

fix: revert INCORRECT_DATA changes per code review

Per Avogar's review: most LOGICAL_ERROR sites changed in the initial
commit are actual programming errors in MergeTree deserialization, not
data-format errors. The `INCORRECT_DATA` pattern is appropriate only
when reading from an explicitly untrusted stream (Native format over the
wire). Changing them unconditionally would silently swallow real bugs
and remove the production alerts on LOGICAL_ERROR.

Reverted files (error codes restored to LOGICAL_ERROR or native_format
conditional):
- SerializationNullable.cpp  — restore native_format ternary
- SerializationTuple.cpp     — restore native_format ternary
- SerializationArray.cpp     — restore LOGICAL_ERROR
- SerializationString.cpp    — restore LOGICAL_ERROR
- SerializationObject.cpp    — restore LOGICAL_ERROR / native_format
- SerializationObjectSharedData.cpp — restore all LOGICAL_ERROR
- SerializationSparse.cpp    — restore LOGICAL_ERROR + max_rows_to_read
  guard (max_rows_to_read && ...) which was also incorrectly changed
- ColumnUnique.h             — restore LOGICAL_ERROR (kept typo fix
  "grated" → "greater" separately)

Also removed the 03925_sparse_values_in_substreams_cache_bug test that
was added to cover the sparse deserialization change, which is also
being reverted.

Remaining changes (kept):
- NativeReader.cpp          — row count mismatch is purely data-driven
- SerializationReplicated.cpp — explicitly endorsed by reviewer (lines
  198 and 224: num_rows != limit, invalid size_of_indexes_type)
- Allocator.cpp             — CANNOT_ALLOCATE_MEMORY for attacker-
  controlled oversized allocation (separate concern, not serialization)
fix: declare LOGICAL_ERROR in SerializationTuple.cpp ErrorCodes block

fix: address Avogar review — native_format ternary, restore sparse test, drop 04038

- `SerializationReplicated`: use `settings.native_format ? INCORRECT_DATA : LOGICAL_ERROR`
  for the `num_rows != limit` and `size_of_indexes_type` checks; revert
  the empty-elements-stream throw back to `LOGICAL_ERROR` (unreachable in
  Native format per Avogar).
- Restore `03925_sparse_values_in_substreams_cache_bug` test that was
  accidentally removed by the prior revert commit.
- Remove `04038_check_table_sparse_tuple_dynamic` test — the underlying
  issue (#96588) is not fixed in this PR and the test fails in CI.

fix: restore SerializationSparse bug fix, revert Allocator/Replicated LOGICAL_ERROR, fix 03925 reference, restore 04038 test

Per Avogar review:
- Revert SerializationSparse.cpp to master: restore limit=0 early return and
  insert_only_rows_in_current_range_from_substreams_cache flag (bug fix for #96588)
- Restore LOGICAL_ERROR in Allocator.cpp checkSize (not reachable via user data)
- Restore LOGICAL_ERROR in SerializationReplicated.cpp (not reachable in Native format)
- Restore deleted regression test 04038_check_table_sparse_tuple_dynamic
- Fix 03925 reference: non-Nullable String.size is 0 not \N, tuple shows '' not NULL
fix: add missing trailing tab in 04038 reference to match CHECK TABLE output format
fix: restore VectorWithMemoryTracking and skipWhitespaceAndSQLComments accidentally dropped during rebase

fix: restore 03925 test to original nullable+sparse form matching master

The test was inadvertently changed from Nullable(String)/Nullable(UInt64) with
nullable_serialization_version = 'allow_sparse' to plain String/UInt64, weakening
the regression coverage for the nullable+sparse substream cache bug.

Since this PR no longer touches SerializationSparse.cpp, the original test form
passes correctly. Restore to match master exactly.

fix: remove unused skipWhitespaceAndSQLComments declaration from ReadHelpers.h

The declaration was added without a corresponding implementation and is
not used anywhere. Remove per code review feedback.

title: Fix abort in DataTypeTuple::createColumn when serialization is SerializationDetached
body:
`DataTypeTuple::createColumn(const ISerialization &)` unwraps `SerializationWrapper` layers in a loop, then requires the result to be a `SerializationTuple`. The loop only peels subclasses of `SerializationWrapper`, but `SerializationDetached` is `final : public ISerialization` and is invisible to it.

When the native format stream provides `has_custom=1` with kind `DETACHED` for a Tuple column, `IDataType::getSerialization` wraps `SerializationTuple` with `SerializationDetached`. The `typeid_cast<SerializationTuple*>` check fails and previously threw `LOGICAL_ERROR`, which in ASan/fuzzer builds calls `abortOnFailedAssertion` before the `catch(...)` in the fuzzer can intercept.

Fix: change to `INCORRECT_DATA` at `DataTypeTuple.cpp:220`.

### Changelog category (leave one):
- Bug Fix (user-visible misbehavior in an official stable release)

### Changelog entry (a user-readable short description of the changes that goes into CHANGELOG.md):

Fix process abort in `Tuple` column deserialization when the serialization kind in the binary stream is `DETACHED`.

### Documentation entry for user-facing changes

- [ ] Documentation is written (mandatory for new features)
author: motsc

commits.message:
fix: change INCORRECT_DATA in DataTypeTuple::createColumn for unexpected serialization

DataTypeTuple::createColumn(const ISerialization &) peels SerializationWrapper
layers in a while loop, then checks for SerializationReplicated, then requires
the result to be a SerializationTuple. The loop only unwraps subclasses of
SerializationWrapper, but SerializationDetached is final : public ISerialization
and is therefore invisible to the loop.

When the native format stream provides has_custom=1 with a DETACHED serialization
kind for a Tuple column, IDataType::getSerialization wraps SerializationTuple with
SerializationDetached. The typeid_cast<SerializationTuple*> check then fails and
previously threw LOGICAL_ERROR, which in ASan/fuzzer builds calls
abortOnFailedAssertion -> abort() before the catch(...) in libFuzzer can intercept.

Fix: change ErrorCodes::LOGICAL_ERROR to ErrorCodes::INCORRECT_DATA at the check
site (DataTypeTuple.cpp:220).

fix: handle SerializationDetached in DataTypeTuple::createColumn

Before this fix, when deserializing a Tuple column with SerializationDetached
(used by the parallel blocks marshalling path), createColumn would fail with
LOGICAL_ERROR because the while loop only peels SerializationWrapper subclasses,
and SerializationDetached extends ISerialization directly.

Fix mirrors the existing SerializationReplicated handling: detect the Detached
wrapper and delegate to createColumn(*nested), creating the inner ColumnTuple.
SerializationDetached::deserializeBinaryBulkWithMultipleStreams then wraps it
in ColumnBLOB as expected.

Also expose getNested() on SerializationDetached (like SerializationReplicated),
and restore LOGICAL_ERROR for the true logic-error path (unknown serialization
type), reverting the previous incorrect INCORRECT_DATA change.

test: add unit test for DataTypeTuple::createColumn with SerializationDetached

The path is triggered when a client connected via the Native TCP protocol
sends a block where a Tuple column has has_custom=1 with DETACHED serialization
kind (2). NativeReader reads the kind byte only when server_revision >=
DBMS_MIN_REVISION_WITH_CUSTOM_SERIALIZATION (54454); this is satisfied by
all current clients.

The existing NativeInputFormat (used by clickhouse-local) passes
server_revision=0 and never reads the has_custom byte, so the bug cannot
be reproduced from a SQL stateless test. A C++ unit test directly constructs
a SerializationDetached-wrapped SerializationTuple and passes it to
DataTypeTuple::createColumn, which is the exact call site that previously
threw LOGICAL_ERROR.
fix: use SerializationDetached::create() factory in unit test (constructor is private)

title: Fix logical error in `arraySymmetricDifference` with nullable complex types
body:
The generic code path in `arrayIntersect.cpp` (used for complex types like tuples) created a Nullable result column from `return_type_with_nulls`, but `prepareArrays` had already stripped the outer Nullable from source columns (tracking the null map separately). This caused a serialization/deserialization mismatch: data serialized without a null-flag byte prefix was deserialized by `ColumnNullable::deserializeAndInsertFromArena` which expects one, corrupting the data stream and reading garbage (2^63) as an allocation size.

The fix strips the outer Nullable with `removeNullable`, consistent with all other code paths (numeric, string, date) which create non-nullable columns and let the later Nullable wrapping handle it.

This should fix fuzzer errors like this ["Logical error: Too large size (A) passed to allocator. It indicates an error."](https://s3.amazonaws.com/clickhouse-test-reports/json.html?PR=100956&sha=8a69e66737794a1ea4f648b125e15ab2acf5c4cb&name_0=PR&name_1=AST+fuzzer+%28arm_asan_ubsan%29).

### Changelog category (leave one):
- Bug Fix (user-visible misbehavior in an official stable release)

### Changelog entry (a [user-readable short description](https://github.com/ClickHouse/ClickHouse/blob/master/docs/changelog_entry_guidelines.md) of the changes that goes into CHANGELOG.md):
Strip Nullable from result column in arrayIntersect and related functions to avoid serialization/deserialization mismatch.
author: george-larionov

commits.message:
Fix logical error in `arraySymmetricDifference` with nullable complex types

The generic code path in `arrayIntersect.cpp` (used for complex types like
tuples) created a Nullable result column from `return_type_with_nulls`, but
`prepareArrays` had already stripped the outer Nullable from source columns
(tracking the null map separately). This caused a serialization/deserialization
mismatch: data serialized without a null-flag byte prefix was deserialized by
`ColumnNullable::deserializeAndInsertFromArena` which expects one, corrupting
the data stream and reading garbage (2^63) as an allocation size.

The fix strips the outer Nullable with `removeNullable`, consistent with all
other code paths (numeric, string, date) which create non-nullable columns
and let the later Nullable wrapping handle it.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

title: cast_keep_nullable when enabled should  not throw when casting Dynamic NULL to Variant
body:
### Changelog category (leave one):
- Bug Fix (user-visible misbehavior in an official stable release)

### Changelog entry (a [user-readable short description](https://github.com/ClickHouse/ClickHouse/blob/master/docs/changelog_entry_guidelines.md) of the changes that goes into CHANGELOG.md):
cast_keep_nullable when enabled will not throw when casting Dynamic NULL to Variant

### Details:

Issue #100793 reports that with cast_keep_nullable=1, casting a Dynamic NULL to Variant throws CANNOT_INSERT_NULL_IN_ORDINARY_COLUMN. This is wrong because Variant holds NULLs natively via NULL_DISCRIMINATOR.

Root cause: the throw_on_null condition uses !result_type->canBeInsideNullable() to mean "can't hold NULL", but canBeInsideNullable() answers a different question ("can be wrapped in Nullable()"). Variant and Dynamic both return false for canBeInsideNullable() yet hold NULLs internally.

The resolver already uses isDynamic (correctly scoped). The fix is to add !isVariant() and !isDynamic() to the throw_on_null condition at both call sites, and add a regression test for the issue reproducer.
author: seva-potapov

title: Fix DROP TABLE hang on Kafka tables after consumer heartbeat error
body:
When a Kafka consumer experiences a heartbeat error followed by a rebalance, `DROP TABLE` can hang indefinitely. The shutdown sequence waits for the streaming task to finish, but the task is stuck inside `CompletedPipelineExecutor::execute` because consumers are blocked in librdkafka's `poll_batch` during the rebalance — and the pipeline has no external cancellation mechanism.

Use `CompletedPipelineExecutor::setCancelCallback` to allow the pipeline to be cancelled promptly when `shutdown_called` is set. The callback checks every 100 ms and triggers pipeline cancellation, letting `streamToViews` return quickly so the shutdown sequence can proceed.

Skip offset commits after cancellation: the cancelled pipeline may not have fully written data to dependent views, so committing offsets would cause data loss. The consumer is marked as dirty instead, and offsets remain uncommitted.

CI report: https://s3.amazonaws.com/clickhouse-test-reports/json.html?REF=master&sha=933424a0835a809944686507ee5c8a40f5d440e4&name_0=MasterCI&name_1=Integration%20tests%20%28amd_asan_ubsan%2C%20db%20disk%2C%20old%20analyzer%2C%202%2F6%29

### Changelog category (leave one):
- Bug Fix (user-visible misbehavior in an official stable release)

### Changelog entry (a [user-readable short description](https://github.com/ClickHouse/ClickHouse/blob/master/docs/changelog_entry_guidelines.md) of the changes that goes into CHANGELOG.md):
Fix `DROP TABLE` hanging indefinitely on Kafka engine tables when consumers are stuck in a rebalance after a heartbeat error.

### Documentation entry for user-facing changes

- [ ] Documentation is written (mandatory for new features)
author: alexey-milovidov

commits.message:
Fix DROP TABLE hang on Kafka tables after consumer heartbeat error

When a Kafka consumer experiences a heartbeat error followed by a
rebalance, `DROP TABLE` can hang indefinitely. The shutdown sequence
waits for the streaming task to finish, but the task is stuck inside
`CompletedPipelineExecutor::execute` because consumers are blocked in
librdkafka's `poll_batch` during the rebalance — and the pipeline has
no external cancellation mechanism.

Use `CompletedPipelineExecutor::setCancelCallback` to allow the
pipeline to be cancelled promptly when `shutdown_called` is set.
The callback checks every 100 ms and triggers pipeline cancellation,
letting `streamToViews` return quickly so the shutdown sequence can
proceed.

Skip offset commits after cancellation: the cancelled pipeline may
not have fully written data to dependent views, so committing offsets
would cause data loss. The consumer is marked as dirty instead, and
offsets remain uncommitted.

https://s3.amazonaws.com/clickhouse-test-reports/json.html?REF=master&sha=933424a0835a809944686507ee5c8a40f5d440e4&name_0=MasterCI&name_1=Integration%20tests%20%28amd_asan_ubsan%2C%20db%20disk%2C%20old%20analyzer%2C%202%2F6%29

title: Support Materialized CTE
body:
### Changelog category (leave one):

- New Feature

### Changelog entry (a [user-readable short description](https://github.com/ClickHouse/ClickHouse/blob/master/docs/changelog_entry_guidelines.md) of the changes that goes into CHANGELOG.md):

Support Materialized CTE. Allow evaluating CTEs only once during query execution and store their results in temporary tables. Closes https://github.com/ClickHouse/ClickHouse/issues/53449.

### Documentation entry for user-facing changes

- [ ] Documentation is written (mandatory for new features)

<!---
Directly edit documentation source files in the "docs" folder with the same pull-request as code changes

or

Add a user-readable short description of the changes that should be added to docs.clickhouse.com below.

At a minimum, the following information should be added (but add more as needed).
- Motivation: Why is this function, table engine, etc. useful to ClickHouse users?

- Parameters: If the feature being added takes arguments, options or is influenced by settings, please list them below with a brief explanation.

- Example use: A query or command.
-->

This PR used one commit and some test queries written by @canhld94 in https://github.com/ClickHouse/ClickHouse/pull/61086. Closes https://github.com/ClickHouse/ClickHouse/pull/61086.

<!-- CURSOR_SUMMARY -->
---

> [!NOTE]
> **High Risk**
> Touches core analyzer/planner/optimizer and execution pipeline to introduce new temp-table materialization paths and a new setting, which can affect query correctness/performance and distributed/parallel replicas behavior.
> 
> **Overview**
> Adds experimental **materialized CTEs** via `WITH name AS MATERIALIZED (subquery)`, guarded by new setting `enable_materialized_cte`.
> 
> The analyzer/planner now tracks `is_materialized` on `QueryNode`/`UnionNode`, parses/formats the new syntax, resolves materialized CTE identifiers by converting them to `TableNode`s backed by temporary `StorageMemory`, forbids correlated materialized CTEs, and errors when combined with `WITH RECURSIVE`.
> 
> Query planning/execution gains CTE materialization steps (`MaterializingCTETransform`, `MaterializingCTEStep`, delayed planning + optimization pass) to run CTE subplans once before the main query, coordinate reuse across sets/PK analysis/distributed queries, and inline single-use materialized CTEs back to avoid external-table conflicts; adds tests and docs for behavior and edge cases.
> 
> <sup>Written by [Cursor Bugbot](https://cursor.com/dashboard?tab=bugbot) for commit 1753b656da0a3252e384e62992282f555f3141cd. This will update automatically on new commits. Configure [here](https://cursor.com/dashboard?tab=bugbot).</sup>
<!-- /CURSOR_SUMMARY -->
author: novikd

title: Fix null pointer dereference in DataTypeDynamic::create() error path
body:
### Changelog category (leave one):
- Bug Fix (user-visible misbehavior in an official stable release)

### Changelog entry (a [user-readable short description](https://github.com/ClickHouse/ClickHouse/blob/master/docs/changelog_entry_guidelines.md) of the changes that goes into CHANGELOG.md):
Fix segmentation fault in DataTypeDynamic::create() when the fuzzer generates a malformed Dynamic type AST

### What the bug is

When `DataTypeDynamic::create()` receives a malformed AST where the LHS of the equals operator is not an `ASTIdentifier` (e.g., `Dynamic(42 = 5)` instead of `Dynamic(max_types = 5)`), the `as<ASTIdentifier>()` cast returns nullptr. The error message at line 84 then calls `identifier->formatForErrorMessage()` on the null pointer, causing a segfault.

This is triggered by the BuzzHouse fuzzer which generates randomized ASTs that bypass parser validation. The crash occurs under TSAN (BuzzHouse amd_tsan) with 3 confirmed hits across 3 unrelated PRs in 30 days (STID: 1320-2f88).

### Stack trace from CI

```
IAST::format() @ IAST.h:319
IAST::formatWithPossiblyHidingSensitiveData() @ IAST.cpp:203
IAST::formatForErrorMessage() @ IAST.cpp:220
DB::create() @ DataTypeDynamic.cpp:84
```

### The fix

Store a reference to the original AST node (`argument->arguments->children[0]`) before the cast, and use it in the error message instead of the null `identifier` pointer.

### Test

Added a GTest (`gtest_DataTypeDynamic_create.cpp`) that directly constructs a malformed AST with a literal where an identifier is expected, and verifies that:
1. The factory throws `UNEXPECTED_AST_STRUCTURE` without crashing
2. Valid `Dynamic(max_types=5)` construction still works correctly

### Documentation entry for user-facing changes

- [ ] Documentation is written (mandatory for new features)

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
author: groeneai

commits.message:
Fix null pointer dereference in DataTypeDynamic::create() error path

When BuzzHouse generates a malformed Dynamic type AST where the LHS
of the equals operator is not an ASTIdentifier (e.g., a literal),
the as<ASTIdentifier>() cast returns nullptr. The error message then
called identifier->formatForErrorMessage() on the null pointer,
causing a segfault (STID: 1320-2f88).

Fix: use the original AST node for the error message instead of the
null cast result.

Fixes: https://github.com/ClickHouse/ClickHouse/issues/101309

commits.message:
Fix undefined behaviour in DateTimeBestEffort parsing

1. Follow same pattern which already existed.
2. Consistently use "if constexpr (strict)"

Add regression test for UB fix in `parseDateTimeBestEffort`

Test that parsing datetime strings with many fractional digits (more than
`Int64::digits10 = 18`) does not cause signed integer overflow in
`readDecimalNumber`. Covers 10-digit and 9-digit unix timestamps with
fractional parts, and regular datetime strings.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

Clarify regression test: add explicit 19-digit case and fix boundary comment

`readDigits` reads up to `UInt64::digits10 = 19` digits into its buffer,
so a 19-digit fractional tail is consumed and truncated to 18 (silently
dropped), while a 20-digit tail leaves the 20th digit in the stream and
causes a parse error. Add the 19-digit case to lock in the expected
truncation behavior and update the comment to reflect the actual boundary.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

Fix regression test to actually reproduce the UB

The previous test used fractional values that fit in Int64 even without the
cap, so the bugfix validator (which runs the test against master) correctly
reported "failed to reproduce the bug".

Use 19 nines as the fractional part: without the cap, `readDecimalNumber`
processes chunks 4+4+4+4+3 and the final step `9999999999999999 * 1000`
overflows Int64. With the fix the count is capped to 18 (chunks 4+4+4+4+2)
and no overflow occurs.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

Merge pull request #100948 from bacek/bacek/datatime-ub

Fix undefined behaviour in DateTimeBestEffort parsing

title: Remove incorrect replaceRegexpOne to extract rewrite; fix exception with group_by_use_nulls
body:
## Summary

The `RegexpFunctionRewritePass` had an optimization (`handleReplaceRegexpOne`) that rewrote `replaceRegexpOne(x, '^(pattern)$', '\1')` to `extract(x, '^(pattern)$')`. This rewrite is semantically incorrect: when the pattern doesn't match, `replaceRegexpOne` returns the original string, while `extract` returns an empty string.

This incorrect rewrite also caused a LOGICAL_ERROR exception when used with `GROUP BY ... WITH CUBE` and `group_by_use_nulls=1`: the `Nullable` wrapper on the result type caused the pass to skip one copy of the expression but rewrite the other, creating a name/type mismatch.

Fix: remove the `replaceRegexpOne` → `extract` optimization entirely. The other rewrites (`replaceRegexpAll` → `replaceRegexpOne`, and `extract` prefix/suffix simplification) are kept since they are semantically correct.

Closes https://github.com/ClickHouse/ClickHouse/issues/88218

### Changelog category (leave one):
- Critical Bug Fix (crash, data loss, RBAC) or LOGICAL_ERROR

### Changelog entry (a [user-readable short description](https://github.com/ClickHouse/ClickHouse/blob/master/docs/changelog_entry_guidelines.md) of the changes that goes into CHANGELOG.md):
Remove incorrect `replaceRegexpOne` to `extract` query rewrite that produced wrong results when the regexp didn't match; also fix exception when `replaceRegexpOne` is used with `GROUP BY ... WITH CUBE` and `group_by_use_nulls=1`.

### Documentation entry for user-facing changes

- [ ] Documentation is written (mandatory for new features)
author: alexey-milovidov

reviews.body: LGTM

commits.message:
Fix exception from `replaceRegexpOne` with `group_by_use_nulls` and CUBE

`RegexpFunctionRewritePass` skipped rewriting `replaceRegexpOne` to
`extract` when the function's result type was `Nullable(String)` (set by
`group_by_use_nulls`), because `isString(Nullable(String))` returned
false. The GROUP BY copy was rewritten (non-nullable), creating a name
mismatch. The planner then re-evaluated the function with non-nullable
inputs but expected a `Nullable(String)` result, causing: "Unexpected
return type from replaceRegexpOne. Expected Nullable(String). Got
Const(String)."

Fix: use `removeNullable` before the `isString` check so the pass
rewrites both copies consistently.

https://github.com/ClickHouse/ClickHouse/issues/88218

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

Remove incorrect `replaceRegexpOne` to `extract` rewrite

The `handleReplaceRegexpOne` optimization rewrote `replaceRegexpOne` to
`extract`, but these functions have different semantics when the pattern
does not match: `replaceRegexpOne` returns the original string, while
`extract` returns an empty string. This produced wrong results.

This also fixes the LOGICAL_ERROR exception from the original issue,
because the inconsistent rewrite (one copy rewritten, the other not)
no longer happens.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
Merge branch 'fix-regexp-rewrite-nullable-group-by' of github.com:ClickHouse/ClickHouse into fix-regexp-rewrite-nullable-group-by

title: Mark support for KQL and PRQL as experimental
body:
ClickHouse supports PRQL and Kusto Query Language (KQL) as alternative query languages but they each have their own problems:
- PRQL: seems not very popular, implemented in Rust ([original PR](https://github.com/ClickHouse/ClickHouse/pull/50686)) which is foreign to ClickHouse's codebase, based on (conceptually) unbeautiful PRQL-to-SQL translation instead of an own parser,
- KQL: https://github.com/ClickHouse/ClickHouse/issues/61742

The [docs](https://clickhouse.com/docs/en/guides/developer/alternative-query-languages) also recognize these issues.

### Changelog category (leave one):
- Improvement

### Changelog entry (a user-readable short description of the changes that goes to CHANGELOG.md):
Support for the alternative query languages PRQL and KQL was marked experimental. To use them, specify settings `allow_experimental_prql_dialect = 1` and `allow_experimental_kusto_dialect = 1`.
author: rschu1ze

commits.message: Mark KQL and PRQL as experimental

title: ClickHouse Client: Autodetect secure connection based on port
body:
ClickHouse Client already autodetects port 9440 if `--secure` is specified, but the reverse is not true. Anecdotally, I see users missing `--secure` every once in a while and not being able to connect because of it. The error message when that happens isn't very helpful either: `Poco::Exception. Code: 1000, e.code() = 22, Invalid argument (version 24.11.1.2557 (official build))`. 

This change implements autodetecting `--secure` if the port is 9440 (the default secure port). The command-line options `--secure` and `--no-secure` still take precedence.

I've also adjusted some of the docs not to specify both `--secure` and `--port 9440`. I chose to keep the port because I think users will generally expect to specify it when connecting to a database - but I don't have a strong opinion, and we could also keep `--secure`.

### Changelog category (leave one):
- Improvement

### Changelog entry (a user-readable short description of the changes that goes to CHANGELOG.md):
Autodetect secure connection based on connecting to port 9440 in ClickHouse Client.
author: cwurm
